content.content:
1вҶ’# Hand Gesture Recognition for Interactive Puzzle Game Control
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’## A Comparative Study of Heuristic, Classical ML, and Deep Learning Approaches
     4вҶ’
     5вҶ’---
     6вҶ’
     7вҶ’## 1. Introduction
     8вҶ’
     9вҶ’This project implements a real-time hand gesture recognition system for controlling a browser-based jigsaw puzzle game via webcam. The system uses Google MediaPipe HandLandmarker to extract 21 three-dimensional hand landmarks from video frames, then classifies these landmarks into five gesture categories using three different classification methods.
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’The three methods compared are:
    12вҶ’1. **Rule-Based Heuristic** -- Hand-crafted threshold rules on geometric features
    13вҶ’2. **Random Forest** -- Classical machine learning with engineered distance features
    14вҶ’3. **MLP Neural Network** -- Deep learning on raw normalized landmarks
    15вҶ’
    16вҶ’### Gesture Classes
    17вҶ’
    18вҶ’| Gesture | Description | Game Action |
    19вҶ’|---------|-------------|-------------|
    20вҶ’| Open Hand | Spread fingers, palm facing camera | Release puzzle piece |
    21вҶ’| Fist | Closed fist | Rotate puzzle piece |
    22вҶ’| Pinch | Thumb and index finger touching | Grab/release puzzle piece |
    23вҶ’| Frame | Two hands forming rectangle | Take screenshot |
    24вҶ’| None | No specific gesture / random position | No action |
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’---
    27вҶ’
    28вҶ’## 2. Data Preparation
    29вҶ’
    30вҶ’### 2.1 Dataset: HaGRID v2
    31вҶ’
    32вҶ’We use the **HaGRID v2** (Hand Gesture Recognition Image Dataset) [1], a large-scale dataset containing over 1 million FullHD RGB images across 33 gesture classes with auto-annotated MediaPipe landmarks.
    33вҶ’
    34вҶ’**Key properties:**
    35вҶ’- 1,086,158 images from 37,583 unique subjects
    36вҶ’- JSON annotation files include pre-computed MediaPipe hand landmarks (x, y coordinates)
    37вҶ’- License: CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 (non-commercial)
    38вҶ’
    39вҶ’### 2.2 Gesture Mapping
    40вҶ’
    41вҶ’We mapped five HaGRID gesture classes to our target gestures:
    42вҶ’
    43вҶ’| Our Class | HaGRID Class | Rationale |
    44вҶ’|-----------|-------------|-----------|
    45вҶ’| open_hand | `stop` | Upright open hand, palm facing camera |
    46вҶ’| fist | `fist` | Direct match -- closed fist |
    47вҶ’| pinch | `thumb_index` | Thumb and index finger touching/close |
    48вҶ’| frame | `take_picture` | Two-hand rectangular frame gesture |
    49вҶ’| none | `no_gesture` | Background/no specific gesture |
    50вҶ’
    51вҶ’### 2.3 Data Extraction
    52вҶ’
    53вҶ’A conversion script (`ml/convert_hagrid.py`) parses the HaGRID JSON annotation files and extracts MediaPipe landmarks into CSV format.
    54вҶ’
    55вҶ’**CSV Schema:** `person_id, gesture_label, timestamp, x0, y0, z0, x1, y1, z1, ..., x20, y20, z20`
    56вҶ’
    57вҶ’**Note:** HaGRID annotations provide only x, y coordinates (2D). The z-coordinate is set to 0.0. This is acceptable because MediaPipe's z-depth estimate is a rough relative depth that contributes minimal discriminative power compared to x, y spatial relationships.
    58вҶ’
    59вҶ’### 2.4 Dataset Statistics
    60вҶ’
    61вҶ’| Class | Train Samples | Val Samples | Total |
    62вҶ’|-------|--------------|-------------|-------|
    63вҶ’| open_hand | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
    64вҶ’| fist | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
    65вҶ’| pinch | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
    66вҶ’| frame | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
    67вҶ’| none | 1,000 | 231 | 1,231 |
    68вҶ’| **Total** | **5,000** | **4,231** | **9,231** |
    69вҶ’
    70вҶ’- **Unique subjects**: 3,607 (from HaGRID `user_id` field)
    71вҶ’- Samples were capped at 1,000 per gesture per split for computational efficiency
    72вҶ’
    73вҶ’### 2.5 Preprocessing Pipeline
    74вҶ’
    75вҶ’The shared preprocessing normalizes raw MediaPipe landmarks to be position- and scale-invariant:
    76вҶ’
    77вҶ’1. **Wrist-relative translation**: Subtract the wrist landmark (index 0) position from all 21 landmarks, centering the hand at the origin
    78вҶ’2. **Scale normalization**: Divide all coordinates by the Euclidean distance from wrist to middle-finger MCP (index 9), making the representation invariant to hand size and camera distance
    79вҶ’3. **Output**: 20 normalized landmarks x 3 coordinates = **60-dimensional feature vector** per sample (wrist becomes origin, excluded)
    80вҶ’
    81вҶ’```
    82вҶ’Raw landmarks (21 x 3) вҶ’ Wrist-relative (21 x 3) вҶ’ Scale-normalized (20 x 3) вҶ’ 60-dim vector
    83вҶ’```
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’---
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’## 3. Feature Extraction and Model Training
    88вҶ’
    89вҶ’### 3.1 Method 1: Rule-Based Heuristic Classifier
    90вҶ’
    91вҶ’**Approach:** Hand-crafted threshold rules operating on geometric features computed from normalized landmarks.
    92вҶ’
    93вҶ’**Features computed:**
    94вҶ’- **Finger curl angles**: Angle between proximal-intermediate and intermediate-distal phalange segments for each finger
    95вҶ’- **Key distances**: Fingertip-to-palm distances, inter-finger distances
    96вҶ’
    97вҶ’**Classification rules:**
    98вҶ’- Open Hand: All fingers extended (curl angles below threshold)
    99вҶ’- Fist: All fingers curled (curl angles above threshold)
   100вҶ’- Pinch: Thumb-tip to index-tip distance below proximity threshold
   101вҶ’- Frame: Two hands detected with specific spatial relationship
   102вҶ’- None: Default when no other rule matches
   103вҶ’
   104вҶ’**Training:** None required -- rules are fixed a priori.
   105вҶ’
   106вҶ’### 3.2 Method 2: Random Forest Classifier
   107вҶ’
   108вҶ’**Approach:** Ensemble of 100 decision trees trained on engineered pairwise distance features.
   109вҶ’
   110вҶ’**Features (24-dimensional):**
   111вҶ’- Pairwise Euclidean distances between key landmark pairs (fingertips, MCP joints, PIP joints)
   112вҶ’- Selected to capture discriminative geometric relationships between fingers
   113вҶ’
   114вҶ’**Model configuration:**
   115вҶ’- `sklearn.ensemble.RandomForestClassifier`
   116вҶ’- `n_estimators=100`, `max_depth=15`
   117вҶ’- Trained using standard scikit-learn API
   118вҶ’
   119вҶ’### 3.3 Method 3: MLP Neural Network
   120вҶ’
   121вҶ’**Approach:** Feed-forward neural network operating directly on the 60-dimensional normalized landmark vector.
   122вҶ’
   123вҶ’**Architecture:**
   124вҶ’
   125вҶ’```
   126вҶ’Input (60) вҶ’ Dense(128, ReLU) вҶ’ Dropout(0.3) вҶ’ Dense(64, ReLU) вҶ’ Dropout(0.3) вҶ’ Dense(5, Softmax)
   127вҶ’```
   128вҶ’
   129вҶ’**Training configuration:**
   130вҶ’- Optimizer: Adam (learning rate = 1e-3)
   131вҶ’- Loss: Sparse categorical cross-entropy
   132вҶ’- Early stopping: patience = 10 epochs, monitoring validation loss
   133вҶ’- Batch size: 32
   134вҶ’- Maximum epochs: 50
   135вҶ’
   136вҶ’**Training result:** Converged in 17 epochs (early stopped), train accuracy = 98.56%, validation accuracy = 97.98%.
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’### 3.4 Methodology Comparison
   139вҶ’
   140вҶ’| Aspect | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP |
   141вҶ’|--------|-----------|---------------|-----|
   142вҶ’| Input Features | Angles + distances | 24 pairwise distances | 60 raw normalized coords |
   143вҶ’| Feature Engineering | Manual (domain expert) | Semi-automatic (selected pairs) | None (end-to-end) |
   144вҶ’| Training Required | No | Yes (fast) | Yes (moderate) |
   145вҶ’| Parameters | ~20 thresholds | ~100K tree nodes | 14,789 weights |
   146вҶ’| Model Size | 170 B | 8.6 MB | 227 KB |
   147вҶ’| Interpretability | High (explicit rules) | Medium (feature importance) | Low (black box) |
   148вҶ’
   149вҶ’---
   150вҶ’
   151вҶ’## 4. Evaluation
   152вҶ’
   153вҶ’### 4.1 Cross-Validation Strategy
   154вҶ’
   155вҶ’We use **Group 5-Fold Cross-Validation** (sklearn `GroupKFold`) with person IDs as groups. This ensures:
   156вҶ’- No subject appears in both training and test sets within any fold
   157вҶ’- The model is evaluated on truly unseen individuals
   158вҶ’- Results reflect generalization to new users
   159вҶ’
   160вҶ’Full Leave-One-Person-Out CV was computationally impractical with 3,607 unique subjects.
   161вҶ’
   162вҶ’Additionally, a **person-aware stratified split** (using `GroupShuffleSplit`) provides a secondary evaluation metric, ensuring no person appears in both train and test sets even in the simple split evaluation.
   163вҶ’
   164вҶ’### 4.2 Overall Results
   165вҶ’
   166вҶ’| Method | CV Accuracy | Std Dev | Stratified Split | Latency (ms) | 95% CI |
   167вҶ’|--------|------------|---------|------------------|-------------|--------|
   168вҶ’| Heuristic | 0.99% | Вұ0.26% | 1.08% | 0.105 | [0.78%, 1.18%] |
   169вҶ’| Random Forest | 94.13% | Вұ1.25% | 95.29% | 13.189 | [93.65%, 94.57%] |
   170вҶ’| **MLP** | **98.41%** | **Вұ0.26%** | **98.75%** | 43.729 | [98.16%, 98.64%] |
   171вҶ’
   172вҶ’### 4.3 Per-Class Results
   173вҶ’
   174вҶ’#### Random Forest (94.13% overall)
   175вҶ’
   176вҶ’| Class | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | Support |
   177вҶ’|-------|-----------|--------|----------|---------|
   178вҶ’| open_hand | 0.984 | 0.986 | 0.985 | 2,000 |
   179вҶ’| fist | 0.976 | 0.990 | 0.983 | 2,000 |
   180вҶ’| pinch | 0.935 | 0.903 | 0.919 | 2,000 |
   181вҶ’| frame | 0.898 | 0.887 | 0.892 | 2,000 |
   182вҶ’| none | 0.898 | 0.941 | 0.919 | 1,231 |
   183вҶ’
   184вҶ’#### MLP (98.41% overall)
   185вҶ’
   186вҶ’| Class | Precision | Recall | F1-Score | Support |
   187вҶ’|-------|-----------|--------|----------|---------|
   188вҶ’| open_hand | 0.989 | 0.999 | 0.994 | 2,000 |
   189вҶ’| fist | 0.992 | 0.994 | 0.993 | 2,000 |
   190вҶ’| pinch | 0.995 | 0.995 | 0.995 | 2,000 |
   191вҶ’| frame | 0.981 | 0.966 | 0.973 | 2,000 |
   192вҶ’| none | 0.951 | 0.956 | 0.953 | 1,231 |
   193вҶ’
   194вҶ’### 4.4 Cross-Validation Fold Stability
   195вҶ’
   196вҶ’| Fold | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP |
   197вҶ’|------|-----------|---------------|-----|
   198вҶ’| 1 | 0.60% | 93.45% | 98.48% |
   199вҶ’| 2 | 0.98% | 94.64% | 97.89% |
   200вҶ’| 3 | 1.30% | 94.75% | 98.54% |
   201вҶ’| 4 | 1.25% | 92.09% | 98.54% |
   202вҶ’| 5 | 0.81% | 95.72% | 98.59% |
   203вҶ’
   204вҶ’The MLP shows remarkably stable performance across folds (std = 0.26%), indicating strong generalization. The Random Forest shows slightly more variance (std = 1.25%), particularly on fold 4.
   205вҶ’
   206вҶ’### 4.5 Error Analysis
   207вҶ’
   208вҶ’#### Why did the Heuristic fail (~1% accuracy)?
   209вҶ’
   210вҶ’The heuristic classifier's rules were designed based on assumptions about normalized landmark distributions that do not match real HaGRID data:
   211вҶ’
   212вҶ’1. **Coordinate range mismatch**: The rules assume specific ranges for finger curl angles computed from 3D landmarks, but HaGRID provides only 2D (x, y) coordinates with z = 0, collapsing the 3D geometric relationships the rules depend on.
   213вҶ’
   214вҶ’2. **Normalization sensitivity**: Wrist-relative normalization followed by scale normalization produces coordinate values in a different range than what the hand-crafted thresholds expect. The rules were calibrated for synthetic data distributions.
   215вҶ’
   216вҶ’3. **No adaptability**: Unlike ML methods that learn from data, the heuristic cannot adapt its decision boundaries to the actual data distribution.
   217вҶ’
   218вҶ’**Lesson:** Rule-based approaches require careful calibration to the specific data pipeline and sensor characteristics. They are brittle to changes in preprocessing or data source.
   219вҶ’
   220вҶ’#### Where does Random Forest struggle?
   221вҶ’
   222вҶ’- **Frame gesture (F1 = 0.892)**: This is a two-handed gesture. Since landmarks are extracted per-hand, the spatial relationship between two hands is lost in the per-hand feature representation. Some frame samples get confused with pinch (both involve finger proximity patterns).
   223вҶ’- **Pinch gesture (F1 = 0.919)**: Subtle differences between pinch (thumb touching index) and other gestures where fingers are close together.
   224вҶ’
   225вҶ’#### Where does MLP struggle?
   226вҶ’
   227вҶ’- **None class (F1 = 0.953)**: The "none" class is inherently diverse (any non-gesture hand position), making it harder to learn a compact decision boundary.
   228вҶ’- **Frame gesture (F1 = 0.973)**: Same two-hand limitation as RF, though MLP handles it better due to richer feature representation.
   229вҶ’
   230вҶ’### 4.6 Accuracy vs. Latency Tradeoff
   231вҶ’
   232вҶ’```
   233вҶ’                    Accuracy
   234вҶ’                    в”Ӯ
   235вҶ’            98.4% в”Җв”Җв”Ө                              в—Ҹ MLP
   236вҶ’                    в”Ӯ
   237вҶ’            94.1% в”Җв”Җв”Ө              в—Ҹ RF
   238вҶ’                    в”Ӯ
   239вҶ’                    в”Ӯ
   240вҶ’             1.0% в”Җв”Җв”Ө  в—Ҹ Heuristic
   241вҶ’                    в”Ӯ
   242вҶ’                    в””в”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җв”¬в”Җв”Җв”Җв”Җ Latency (ms)
   243вҶ’                       0.1       13.2       43.7
   244вҶ’```
   245вҶ’
   246вҶ’- **Heuristic**: Fastest (0.1 ms) but non-functional
   247вҶ’- **Random Forest**: Good balance (94% accuracy, 13 ms latency) -- suitable for real-time at 60 FPS (16.7 ms budget)
   248вҶ’- **MLP**: Highest accuracy (98.4%) but 43.7 ms latency -- suitable for 20 FPS inference
   249вҶ’
   250вҶ’For the puzzle game application, the Random Forest provides the best practical tradeoff: near-real-time performance with >94% accuracy. The MLP can be used if inference is batched or FPS requirements are relaxed.
   251вҶ’

   316вҶ’## 6. Conclusion
   317вҶ’
   318вҶ’### 6.1 Summary of Findings
   319вҶ’
   320вҶ’| Criterion | Best Method | Score |
   321вҶ’|-----------|------------|-------|
   322вҶ’| Overall Accuracy | MLP | 98.41% |
   323вҶ’| Inference Speed | Heuristic | 0.1 ms |
   324вҶ’| Accuracy/Latency Balance | Random Forest | 94.13% @ 13 ms |
   325вҶ’| Per-Class Consistency | MLP | All classes >95% F1 |
   326вҶ’| Model Size | Heuristic | 170 B |
   327вҶ’| Generalization (low std) | MLP | Вұ0.26% |
   328вҶ’
   329вҶ’The **MLP Neural Network** is the clear winner for classification accuracy, achieving 98.41% on Group 5-Fold CV with tight confidence intervals. It outperforms Random Forest by 4.3 percentage points across all gesture classes.
   330вҶ’
   331вҶ’The **Random Forest** offers the best practical tradeoff for real-time applications, with 94.13% accuracy and latency well within the 16.7ms budget for 60 FPS rendering.
   332вҶ’
   333вҶ’The **Heuristic** approach, while fastest, demonstrates the fundamental limitation of hand-crafted rules: they are brittle to changes in data distribution and require manual recalibration for each deployment context.
   334вҶ’
   335вҶ’### 6.2 Recommendations
   336вҶ’
   337вҶ’For the jigsaw puzzle game application, we recommend:
   338вҶ’1. **Use the MLP model** for gesture classification (98.4% accuracy, 44ms latency is acceptable for game control)
   339вҶ’2. **Add 3-frame temporal smoothing** to reduce jitter and handle the ~2% error rate
   340вҶ’3. **Set confidence threshold at 0.7** to prevent low-confidence misclassifications
   341вҶ’
   342вҶ’### 6.3 Code Quality and Testing
   343вҶ’
   344вҶ’The project includes **28 automated unit tests** (`ml/tests/test_core.py`) covering:
   345вҶ’
   346вҶ’- **Normalization invariance**: Translation invariance, scale invariance, degenerate inputs, batch consistency
   347вҶ’- **Feature extraction correctness**: Angle key/range validation, known-geometry verification, distance non-negativity
   348вҶ’- **Classifier contracts**: Predict returns valid labels, proba shapes and sum-to-one, RF save/load roundtrip
   349вҶ’- **Evaluation framework**: Confusion matrix shape, bootstrap CI range, LOPO-CV execution, person-aware stratified split
   350вҶ’
   351вҶ’Additional engineering improvements:
   352вҶ’- **Single source of truth**: `GESTURE_CLASSES` defined once in `preprocessing.py`, imported everywhere
   353вҶ’- **Person-aware evaluation**: `stratified_split` uses `GroupShuffleSplit` to prevent data leakage
   354вҶ’- **Reproducible dependencies**: `requirements.txt` includes version pinning and all dependencies (including `mediapipe`, `opencv-python`)
   355вҶ’- **Flexible MLP training**: `MLPClassifier.train()` accepts optional `validation_data` parameter for person-aware validation during early stopping
   356вҶ’
   357вҶ’### 6.4 Future Work
   358вҶ’
   359вҶ’1. **Heuristic recalibration**: Redesign rules for 2D landmark distributions or re-extract landmarks with z-coordinates using the MediaPipe Python API directly on HaGRID images
   360вҶ’2. **Frame gesture improvement**: Implement the two-stage frame detector that analyzes the spatial relationship between both hands, rather than classifying each hand independently
   361вҶ’3. **Model quantization**: Convert the MLP to TFLite or ONNX for faster browser inference via WebAssembly
   362вҶ’4. **User adaptation**: Fine-tune models on the specific user's hand geometry for improved personalization
   363вҶ’5. **Temporal modeling**: Use LSTM or 1D-CNN over sequences of landmark frames for gesture recognition that accounts for motion patterns
   364вҶ’

   388вҶ’## Appendix B: Reproduction
   389вҶ’
   390вҶ’```bash
   391вҶ’# 1. Install dependencies
   392вҶ’pip install -r ml/requirements.txt
   393вҶ’
   394вҶ’# 2. Download and extract HaGRID annotations
   395вҶ’curl -L -o data/annotations.zip \
   396вҶ’  "https://rndml-team-cv.obs.ru-moscow-1.hc.sbercloud.ru/datasets/hagrid_v2/annotations_with_landmarks/annotations.zip"
   397вҶ’unzip data/annotations.zip -d data/annotations
   398вҶ’
   399вҶ’# 3. Convert HaGRID to CSV (1000 samples per gesture)
   400вҶ’cd ml/
   401вҶ’python convert_hagrid.py --annotations_dir ../data/annotations/annotations \
   402вҶ’  --output ../data/hagrid_landmarks.csv --max_per_gesture 1000 --splits train val
   403вҶ’
   404вҶ’# 4. Train and evaluate all methods
   405вҶ’python train.py --data_dir ../data --output_dir ../models --epochs 50
   406вҶ’
   407вҶ’# 5. Results in models/evaluation_report.txt and models/plots/
   408вҶ’```

content.content:
1вҶ’"""Training orchestrator for hand gesture classification pipeline.
     2вҶ’
     3вҶ’Loads data, preprocesses, trains all three classifiers (Heuristic,
     4вҶ’Random Forest, MLP), runs full evaluation (LOPO-CV + stratified split),
     5вҶ’generates comparison plots, and saves models.
     6вҶ’
     7вҶ’Usage:
     8вҶ’    python train.py --data_dir ../data --output_dir ../models
     9вҶ’
    10вҶ’If no CSV data exists, generates synthetic sample data automatically.
    11вҶ’"""
    12вҶ’
    13вҶ’from __future__ import annotations
    14вҶ’
    15вҶ’import argparse
    16вҶ’import os
    17вҶ’import sys
    18вҶ’import time
    19вҶ’from typing import Any
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’import numpy as np
    22вҶ’
    23вҶ’# Add the ml directory to path for imports
    24вҶ’sys.path.insert(0, os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
    25вҶ’
    26вҶ’from preprocessing import (
    27вҶ’    GESTURE_CLASSES,
    28вҶ’    generate_sample_csv,
    29вҶ’    load_data,
    30вҶ’    preprocess_dataset,
    31вҶ’)
    32вҶ’from features import (
    33вҶ’    compute_heuristic_features_batch,
    34вҶ’    compute_mlp_features,
    35вҶ’    compute_pairwise_distances_batch,
    36вҶ’)
    37вҶ’from heuristic import HeuristicClassifier
    38вҶ’from random_forest import RFClassifier
    39вҶ’from mlp import MLPClassifier
    40вҶ’from evaluate import Evaluator
    41вҶ’
    42вҶ’
    43вҶ’def find_csv_files(data_dir: str) -> list[str]:
    44вҶ’    """Find all CSV files in the data directory.
    45вҶ’
    46вҶ’    Args:
    47вҶ’        data_dir: Path to data directory.
    48вҶ’
    49вҶ’    Returns:
    50вҶ’        List of CSV file paths.
    51вҶ’    """
    52вҶ’    csv_files = []
    53вҶ’    if os.path.exists(data_dir):
    54вҶ’        for fname in os.listdir(data_dir):
    55вҶ’            if fname.endswith(".csv"):
    56вҶ’                csv_files.append(os.path.join(data_dir, fname))
    57вҶ’    return sorted(csv_files)
    58вҶ’
    59вҶ’
    60вҶ’def train_heuristic(
    61вҶ’    X: np.ndarray,
    62вҶ’    y: np.ndarray,
    63вҶ’    person_ids: np.ndarray,
    64вҶ’    evaluator: Evaluator,
    65вҶ’    output_dir: str,
    66вҶ’    use_group_kfold: bool = False,
    67вҶ’) -> dict[str, Any]:
    68вҶ’    """Train and evaluate the heuristic classifier.
    69вҶ’
    70вҶ’    Args:
    71вҶ’        X: Normalized landmarks (n_samples, 60).
    72вҶ’        y: Labels.
    73вҶ’        person_ids: Person IDs for LOPO-CV.
    74вҶ’        evaluator: Evaluator instance.
    75вҶ’        output_dir: Directory for saving outputs.
    76вҶ’
    77вҶ’    Returns:
    78вҶ’        Evaluation results dictionary.
    79вҶ’    """
    80вҶ’    print("\n" + "=" * 60)
    81вҶ’    print("METHOD 1: HEURISTIC CLASSIFIER")
    82вҶ’    print("=" * 60)
    83вҶ’
    84вҶ’    clf = HeuristicClassifier()
    85вҶ’
    86вҶ’    # LOPO-CV (heuristic needs no training)
    87вҶ’    def train_predict_fn(
    88вҶ’        X_train: np.ndarray, y_train: np.ndarray, X_test: np.ndarray
    89вҶ’    ) -> np.ndarray:
    90вҶ’        return np.array(clf.predict(X_test))
    91вҶ’
    92вҶ’    print("\nRunning cross-validation...")
    93вҶ’    if use_group_kfold:
    94вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.group_kfold_cv(train_predict_fn, X, y, person_ids)
    95вҶ’    else:
    96вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.lopo_cv(train_predict_fn, X, y, person_ids)
    97вҶ’    print(
    98вҶ’        f"CV Accuracy: {lopo_results['mean_accuracy']:.4f} "
    99вҶ’        f"+/- {lopo_results['std_accuracy']:.4f}"
   100вҶ’    )
   101вҶ’
   102вҶ’    # Stratified split (person-aware)
   103вҶ’    print("Running stratified split evaluation...")
   104вҶ’    split_results = evaluator.stratified_split(train_predict_fn, X, y, groups=person_ids)
   105вҶ’    print(f"Stratified Split Accuracy: {split_results['accuracy']:.4f}")
   106вҶ’
   107вҶ’    # Inference latency
   108вҶ’    latency = evaluator.inference_latency(clf, X[0])
   109вҶ’    print(f"Inference Latency: {latency:.3f} ms")
   110вҶ’
   111вҶ’    # Bootstrap CI
   112вҶ’    point, lower, upper = evaluator.bootstrap_ci(
   113вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   114вҶ’    )
   115вҶ’    print(f"95% CI: {point:.4f} [{lower:.4f}, {upper:.4f}]")
   116вҶ’
   117вҶ’    # Confusion matrix plot
   118вҶ’    cm = evaluator.compute_confusion_matrix(
   119вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   120вҶ’    )
   121вҶ’    evaluator.plot_confusion_matrix(
   122вҶ’        cm,
   123вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "heuristic_confusion_matrix.png"),
   124вҶ’        title="Heuristic Classifier - Confusion Matrix",
   125вҶ’    )
   126вҶ’
   127вҶ’    # Save model parameters
   128вҶ’    clf.save(os.path.join(output_dir, "heuristic_params.json"))
   129вҶ’
   130вҶ’    return {
   131вҶ’        **lopo_results,
   132вҶ’        "latency_ms": latency,
   133вҶ’        "bootstrap_ci": (point, lower, upper),
   134вҶ’        "split_accuracy": split_results["accuracy"],
   135вҶ’    }
   136вҶ’
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’def train_random_forest(
   139вҶ’    X: np.ndarray,
   140вҶ’    y: np.ndarray,
   141вҶ’    person_ids: np.ndarray,
   142вҶ’    evaluator: Evaluator,
   143вҶ’    output_dir: str,
   144вҶ’    use_group_kfold: bool = False,
   145вҶ’) -> dict[str, Any]:
   146вҶ’    """Train and evaluate the Random Forest classifier.
   147вҶ’
   148вҶ’    Args:
   149вҶ’        X: Normalized landmarks (n_samples, 60).
   150вҶ’        y: Labels.
   151вҶ’        person_ids: Person IDs for LOPO-CV.
   152вҶ’        evaluator: Evaluator instance.
   153вҶ’        output_dir: Directory for saving outputs.
   154вҶ’
   155вҶ’    Returns:
   156вҶ’        Evaluation results dictionary.
   157вҶ’    """
   158вҶ’    print("\n" + "=" * 60)
   159вҶ’    print("METHOD 2: RANDOM FOREST CLASSIFIER")
   160вҶ’    print("=" * 60)
   161вҶ’
   162вҶ’    # Extract pairwise distance features
   163вҶ’    print("Extracting pairwise distance features...")
   164вҶ’    X_dist, feat_names = compute_pairwise_distances_batch(X)
   165вҶ’    print(f"Feature vector dimension: {X_dist.shape[1]}")
   166вҶ’
   167вҶ’    # LOPO-CV
   168вҶ’    def train_predict_fn(
   169вҶ’        X_train: np.ndarray, y_train: np.ndarray, X_test: np.ndarray
   170вҶ’    ) -> np.ndarray:
   171вҶ’        clf = RFClassifier()
   172вҶ’        clf.train(X_train, y_train, feat_names)
   173вҶ’        return clf.predict(X_test)
   174вҶ’
   175вҶ’    print("\nRunning cross-validation...")
   176вҶ’    if use_group_kfold:
   177вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.group_kfold_cv(train_predict_fn, X_dist, y, person_ids)
   178вҶ’    else:
   179вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.lopo_cv(train_predict_fn, X_dist, y, person_ids)
   180вҶ’    print(
   181вҶ’        f"CV Accuracy: {lopo_results['mean_accuracy']:.4f} "
   182вҶ’        f"+/- {lopo_results['std_accuracy']:.4f}"
   183вҶ’    )
   184вҶ’
   185вҶ’    # Stratified split (person-aware)
   186вҶ’    print("Running stratified split evaluation...")
   187вҶ’    split_results = evaluator.stratified_split(train_predict_fn, X_dist, y, groups=person_ids)
   188вҶ’    print(f"Stratified Split Accuracy: {split_results['accuracy']:.4f}")
   189вҶ’
   190вҶ’    # Train final model on all data for saving
   191вҶ’    print("Training final model on all data...")
   192вҶ’    final_clf = RFClassifier()
   193вҶ’    train_metrics = final_clf.train(X_dist, y, feat_names)
   194вҶ’    print(f"Final training accuracy: {train_metrics['train_accuracy']:.4f}")
   195вҶ’
   196вҶ’    # Inference latency
   197вҶ’    latency = evaluator.inference_latency(final_clf, X_dist[0])
   198вҶ’    print(f"Inference Latency: {latency:.3f} ms")
   199вҶ’
   200вҶ’    # Bootstrap CI
   201вҶ’    point, lower, upper = evaluator.bootstrap_ci(
   202вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   203вҶ’    )
   204вҶ’    print(f"95% CI: {point:.4f} [{lower:.4f}, {upper:.4f}]")
   205вҶ’
   206вҶ’    # Confusion matrix plot
   207вҶ’    cm = evaluator.compute_confusion_matrix(
   208вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   209вҶ’    )
   210вҶ’    evaluator.plot_confusion_matrix(
   211вҶ’        cm,
   212вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "rf_confusion_matrix.png"),
   213вҶ’        title="Random Forest - Confusion Matrix",
   214вҶ’    )
   215вҶ’
   216вҶ’    # Feature importance plot
   217вҶ’    importances = final_clf.get_feature_importance()
   218вҶ’    evaluator.plot_feature_importance(
   219вҶ’        importances,
   220вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "rf_feature_importance.png"),
   221вҶ’        title="Random Forest - Feature Importance",
   222вҶ’    )
   223вҶ’
   224вҶ’    # Save model
   225вҶ’    final_clf.save(os.path.join(output_dir, "random_forest.joblib"))
   226вҶ’
   227вҶ’    return {
   228вҶ’        **lopo_results,
   229вҶ’        "latency_ms": latency,
   230вҶ’        "bootstrap_ci": (point, lower, upper),
   231вҶ’        "split_accuracy": split_results["accuracy"],
   232вҶ’        "feature_importance": importances,
   233вҶ’    }
   234вҶ’
   235вҶ’
   236вҶ’def train_mlp(
   237вҶ’    X: np.ndarray,
   238вҶ’    y: np.ndarray,
   239вҶ’    person_ids: np.ndarray,
   240вҶ’    evaluator: Evaluator,
   241вҶ’    output_dir: str,
   242вҶ’    epochs: int = 100,
   243вҶ’    verbose: int = 0,
   244вҶ’    use_group_kfold: bool = False,
   245вҶ’) -> dict[str, Any]:
   246вҶ’    """Train and evaluate the MLP classifier.
   247вҶ’
   248вҶ’    Args:
   249вҶ’        X: Normalized landmarks (n_samples, 60).
   250вҶ’        y: Labels.
   251вҶ’        person_ids: Person IDs for LOPO-CV.
   252вҶ’        evaluator: Evaluator instance.
   253вҶ’        output_dir: Directory for saving outputs.
   254вҶ’        epochs: Maximum training epochs.
   255вҶ’        verbose: Keras verbosity level.
   256вҶ’
   257вҶ’    Returns:
   258вҶ’        Evaluation results dictionary.
   259вҶ’    """
   260вҶ’    print("\n" + "=" * 60)
   261вҶ’    print("METHOD 3: MLP NEURAL NETWORK")
   262вҶ’    print("=" * 60)
   263вҶ’
   264вҶ’    # Suppress TF warnings
   265вҶ’    os.environ["TF_CPP_MIN_LOG_LEVEL"] = "2"
   266вҶ’
   267вҶ’    X_mlp = compute_mlp_features(X)  # Same as X, just for consistency
   268вҶ’    print(f"Input dimension: {X_mlp.shape[1]}")
   269вҶ’
   270вҶ’    # LOPO-CV
   271вҶ’    # Note: MLP uses random validation_split for early stopping within each
   272вҶ’    # fold. The final evaluation uses the person-held-out test fold, so the
   273вҶ’    # early stopping split is a minor pragmatic compromise, not a data leak
   274вҶ’    # in the evaluation metric.
   275вҶ’    def train_predict_fn(
   276вҶ’        X_train: np.ndarray, y_train: np.ndarray, X_test: np.ndarray
   277вҶ’    ) -> np.ndarray:
   278вҶ’        clf = MLPClassifier()
   279вҶ’        clf.train(X_train, y_train, epochs=epochs, verbose=0, patience=10)
   280вҶ’        return clf.predict(X_test)
   281вҶ’
   282вҶ’    print("\nRunning cross-validation (this may take a while)...")
   283вҶ’    if use_group_kfold:
   284вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.group_kfold_cv(train_predict_fn, X_mlp, y, person_ids)
   285вҶ’    else:
   286вҶ’        lopo_results = evaluator.lopo_cv(train_predict_fn, X_mlp, y, person_ids)
   287вҶ’    print(
   288вҶ’        f"CV Accuracy: {lopo_results['mean_accuracy']:.4f} "
   289вҶ’        f"+/- {lopo_results['std_accuracy']:.4f}"
   290вҶ’    )
   291вҶ’
   292вҶ’    # Stratified split (person-aware)
   293вҶ’    print("Running stratified split evaluation...")
   294вҶ’    split_results = evaluator.stratified_split(train_predict_fn, X_mlp, y, groups=person_ids)
   295вҶ’    print(f"Stratified Split Accuracy: {split_results['accuracy']:.4f}")
   296вҶ’
   297вҶ’    # Train final model on all data
   298вҶ’    print("Training final model on all data...")
   299вҶ’    final_clf = MLPClassifier()
   300вҶ’    train_metrics = final_clf.train(
   301вҶ’        X_mlp, y, epochs=epochs, verbose=verbose, patience=10
   302вҶ’    )
   303вҶ’    print(f"Final training metrics: {train_metrics}")
   304вҶ’
   305вҶ’    # Inference latency
   306вҶ’    latency = evaluator.inference_latency(final_clf, X_mlp[0])
   307вҶ’    print(f"Inference Latency: {latency:.3f} ms")
   308вҶ’
   309вҶ’    # Bootstrap CI
   310вҶ’    point, lower, upper = evaluator.bootstrap_ci(
   311вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   312вҶ’    )
   313вҶ’    print(f"95% CI: {point:.4f} [{lower:.4f}, {upper:.4f}]")
   314вҶ’
   315вҶ’    # Confusion matrix plot
   316вҶ’    cm = evaluator.compute_confusion_matrix(
   317вҶ’        lopo_results["all_y_true"], lopo_results["all_y_pred"]
   318вҶ’    )
   319вҶ’    evaluator.plot_confusion_matrix(
   320вҶ’        cm,
   321вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "mlp_confusion_matrix.png"),
   322вҶ’        title="MLP - Confusion Matrix",
   323вҶ’    )
   324вҶ’
   325вҶ’    # Save model
   326вҶ’    final_clf.save(os.path.join(output_dir, "mlp_model"))
   327вҶ’
   328вҶ’    # Export ONNX
   329вҶ’    try:
   330вҶ’        final_clf.export_onnx(os.path.join(output_dir, "mlp_model.onnx"))
   331вҶ’        print("ONNX export: OK")
   332вҶ’    except Exception as e:
   333вҶ’        print(f"ONNX export failed (non-critical): {e}")
   334вҶ’
   335вҶ’    return {
   336вҶ’        **lopo_results,
   337вҶ’        "latency_ms": latency,
   338вҶ’        "bootstrap_ci": (point, lower, upper),
   339вҶ’        "split_accuracy": split_results["accuracy"],
   340вҶ’        "train_metrics": train_metrics,
   341вҶ’    }
   342вҶ’
   343вҶ’
   344вҶ’def print_summary_table(results: dict[str, dict[str, Any]]) -> None:
   345вҶ’    """Print a formatted summary comparison table.
   346вҶ’
   347вҶ’    Args:
   348вҶ’        results: Dictionary mapping method names to result dicts.
   349вҶ’    """
   350вҶ’    print("\n" + "=" * 80)
   351вҶ’    print("SUMMARY COMPARISON")
   352вҶ’    print("=" * 80)
   353вҶ’    header = (
   354вҶ’        f"{'Method':<20} {'LOPO Acc':<12} {'Std':<10} "
   355вҶ’        f"{'Split Acc':<12} {'Latency':<12} {'95% CI':<20}"
   356вҶ’    )
   357вҶ’    print(header)
   358вҶ’    print("-" * 80)
   359вҶ’
   360вҶ’    for method, r in results.items():
   361вҶ’        acc = r.get("mean_accuracy", r.get("accuracy", 0))
   362вҶ’        std = r.get("std_accuracy", 0)
   363вҶ’        split_acc = r.get("split_accuracy", 0)
   364вҶ’        latency = r.get("latency_ms", 0)
   365вҶ’        ci = r.get("bootstrap_ci", (0, 0, 0))
   366вҶ’
   367вҶ’        print(
   368вҶ’            f"{method:<20} {acc:<12.4f} {std:<10.4f} "
   369вҶ’            f"{split_acc:<12.4f} {latency:<12.3f} "
   370вҶ’            f"[{ci[1]:.4f}, {ci[2]:.4f}]"
   371вҶ’        )
   372вҶ’
   373вҶ’    print("=" * 80)
   374вҶ’
   375вҶ’
   376вҶ’def main() -> None:
   377вҶ’    """Main training orchestrator entry point."""
   378вҶ’    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser(
   379вҶ’        description="Train and evaluate gesture classifiers"
   380вҶ’    )
   381вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   382вҶ’        "--data_dir",
   383вҶ’        type=str,
   384вҶ’        default=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "data"),
   385вҶ’        help="Directory containing CSV data files",
   386вҶ’    )
   387вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   388вҶ’        "--output_dir",
   389вҶ’        type=str,
   390вҶ’        default=os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "models"),
   391вҶ’        help="Directory for saving models and plots",
   392вҶ’    )
   393вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   394вҶ’        "--epochs",
   395вҶ’        type=int,
   396вҶ’        default=100,
   397вҶ’        help="Maximum training epochs for MLP",
   398вҶ’    )
   399вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   400вҶ’        "--generate_sample",
   401вҶ’        action="store_true",
   402вҶ’        help="Generate synthetic sample data if no CSV files found",
   403вҶ’    )
   404вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   405вҶ’        "--n_persons",
   406вҶ’        type=int,
   407вҶ’        default=5,
   408вҶ’        help="Number of persons for sample data generation",
   409вҶ’    )
   410вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   411вҶ’        "--samples_per_gesture",
   412вҶ’        type=int,
   413вҶ’        default=30,
   414вҶ’        help="Samples per gesture per person for sample data",
   415вҶ’    )
   416вҶ’    parser.add_argument(
   417вҶ’        "--verbose",
   418вҶ’        type=int,
   419вҶ’        default=1,
   420вҶ’        help="Verbosity level (0=silent, 1=normal, 2=detailed)",
   421вҶ’    )
   422вҶ’
   423вҶ’    args = parser.parse_args()
   424вҶ’    data_dir = os.path.abspath(args.data_dir)
   425вҶ’    output_dir = os.path.abspath(args.output_dir)
   426вҶ’
   427вҶ’    # Create output directories
   428вҶ’    os.makedirs(output_dir, exist_ok=True)
   429вҶ’    os.makedirs(os.path.join(output_dir, "plots"), exist_ok=True)
   430вҶ’
   431вҶ’    # ================================================================
   432вҶ’    # Step 1: Load or generate data
   433вҶ’    # ================================================================
   434вҶ’    print("=" * 60)
   435вҶ’    print("GESTURE CLASSIFICATION PIPELINE")
   436вҶ’    print("=" * 60)
   437вҶ’
   438вҶ’    csv_files = find_csv_files(data_dir)
   439вҶ’
   440вҶ’    if not csv_files:
   441вҶ’        if args.generate_sample:
   442вҶ’            print("\nNo CSV files found. Generating synthetic sample data...")
   443вҶ’            os.makedirs(data_dir, exist_ok=True)
   444вҶ’            csv_path = os.path.join(data_dir, "sample_gestures.csv")
   445вҶ’            generate_sample_csv(
   446вҶ’                csv_path,
   447вҶ’                n_persons=args.n_persons,
   448вҶ’                samples_per_gesture=args.samples_per_gesture,
   449вҶ’            )
   450вҶ’            csv_files = [csv_path]
   451вҶ’        else:
   452вҶ’            print(f"\nNo CSV files found in {data_dir}")
   453вҶ’            print("Use --generate_sample to create synthetic data for testing.")
   454вҶ’            sys.exit(1)
   455вҶ’
   456вҶ’    print(f"\nData files: {csv_files}")
   457вҶ’
   458вҶ’    # Load and concatenate all CSV files
   459вҶ’    import pandas as pd
   460вҶ’
   461вҶ’    dfs = [load_data(f) for f in csv_files]
   462вҶ’    df = pd.concat(dfs, ignore_index=True)
   463вҶ’    print(f"Total samples: {len(df)}")
   464вҶ’    print(f"Gesture distribution:\n{df['gesture_label'].value_counts().to_string()}")
   465вҶ’    print(f"Unique persons: {df['person_id'].nunique()}")
   466вҶ’
   467вҶ’    # ================================================================
   468вҶ’    # Step 2: Preprocess
   469вҶ’    # ================================================================
   470вҶ’    print("\nPreprocessing landmarks...")
   471вҶ’    X, y, person_ids = preprocess_dataset(df)
   472вҶ’    print(f"Normalized feature shape: {X.shape}")
   473вҶ’
   474вҶ’    # ================================================================
   475вҶ’    # Step 3: Train and evaluate all methods
   476вҶ’    # ================================================================
   477вҶ’    evaluator = Evaluator()
   478вҶ’    all_results: dict[str, dict[str, Any]] = {}
   479вҶ’
   480вҶ’    # Choose CV strategy based on number of unique persons
   481вҶ’    n_persons = len(np.unique(person_ids))
   482вҶ’    MAX_LOPO_PERSONS = 20
   483вҶ’    use_group_kfold = n_persons > MAX_LOPO_PERSONS
   484вҶ’    if use_group_kfold:
   485вҶ’        cv_name = f"Group 5-Fold CV ({n_persons} persons)"
   486вҶ’        print(f"\nCV Strategy: {cv_name} (too many persons for LOPO)")
   487вҶ’    else:
   488вҶ’        cv_name = f"LOPO-CV ({n_persons} persons)"
   489вҶ’        print(f"\nCV Strategy: {cv_name}")
   490вҶ’
   491вҶ’    start_time = time.time()
   492вҶ’
   493вҶ’    # Method 1: Heuristic
   494вҶ’    all_results["Heuristic"] = train_heuristic(
   495вҶ’        X, y, person_ids, evaluator, output_dir, use_group_kfold=use_group_kfold
   496вҶ’    )
   497вҶ’
   498вҶ’    # Method 2: Random Forest
   499вҶ’    all_results["Random Forest"] = train_random_forest(
   500вҶ’        X, y, person_ids, evaluator, output_dir, use_group_kfold=use_group_kfold
   501вҶ’    )
   502вҶ’
   503вҶ’    # Method 3: MLP
   504вҶ’    all_results["MLP"] = train_mlp(
   505вҶ’        X, y, person_ids, evaluator, output_dir,
   506вҶ’        epochs=args.epochs,
   507вҶ’        verbose=args.verbose,
   508вҶ’        use_group_kfold=use_group_kfold,
   509вҶ’    )
   510вҶ’
   511вҶ’    total_time = time.time() - start_time
   512вҶ’
   513вҶ’    # ================================================================
   514вҶ’    # Step 4: Generate comparison plots
   515вҶ’    # ================================================================
   516вҶ’    print("\nGenerating comparison plots...")
   517вҶ’
   518вҶ’    evaluator.plot_accuracy_comparison(
   519вҶ’        all_results,
   520вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "accuracy_comparison.png"),
   521вҶ’        title="Gesture Classification - Method Comparison",
   522вҶ’    )
   523вҶ’
   524вҶ’    evaluator.plot_per_class_f1(
   525вҶ’        all_results,
   526вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "plots", "per_class_f1_comparison.png"),
   527вҶ’        title="Gesture Classification - Per-Class F1 Comparison",
   528вҶ’    )
   529вҶ’
   530вҶ’    # ================================================================
   531вҶ’    # Step 5: Generate report
   532вҶ’    # ================================================================
   533вҶ’    evaluator.generate_report(
   534вҶ’        all_results,
   535вҶ’        save_path=os.path.join(output_dir, "evaluation_report.txt"),
   536вҶ’    )
   537вҶ’
   538вҶ’    # ================================================================
   539вҶ’    # Step 6: Summary
   540вҶ’    # ================================================================
   541вҶ’    print_summary_table(all_results)
   542вҶ’    print(f"\nTotal pipeline time: {total_time:.1f} seconds")
   543вҶ’    print(f"\nModels saved to: {output_dir}")
   544вҶ’    print(f"Plots saved to: {os.path.join(output_dir, 'plots')}")
   545вҶ’
   546вҶ’
   547вҶ’if __name__ == "__main__":
   548вҶ’    main()
   549вҶ’

content.content:
1вҶ’# Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
     2вҶ’## Presentation Outline (15-20 minutes, 18 slides)
     3вҶ’
     4вҶ’---
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’## Slide 1: Title Slide
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’**Title:** Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
     9вҶ’**Subtitle:** A Comparative Study of Heuristic, Classical ML, and Deep Learning Approaches
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’**Key Elements:**
    12вҶ’- Course: Computer Vision
    13вҶ’- Student Name / Student ID
    14вҶ’- Date: February 2026
    15вҶ’- University logo (if applicable)
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’**Visual:** Clean title layout with a composite image showing the five gesture classes (open hand, fist, pinch, frame, none) as small icons or photos across the bottom.
    18вҶ’
    19вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Today I will present a hand gesture recognition system that lets you control a browser-based jigsaw puzzle using just your webcam. We compare three classification methods of increasing complexity and show why data-driven approaches dominate hand-crafted rules."
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’---
    22вҶ’
    23вҶ’## Slide 2: Problem Statement and Motivation
    24вҶ’
    25вҶ’**Title:** Why Hand Gesture Recognition?
    26вҶ’
    27вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    28вҶ’- Human-computer interaction is moving beyond keyboards and mice toward natural, touchless interfaces
    29вҶ’- Hand gestures are intuitive, require no hardware beyond a webcam, and map naturally to spatial tasks like puzzle manipulation
    30вҶ’- Challenge: Gestures vary dramatically across individuals (hand size, skin tone, angle, lighting)
    31вҶ’- Our goal: Build a real-time gesture classifier that generalizes to unseen users
    32вҶ’
    33вҶ’**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left: photo of someone using a keyboard (traditional). Right: photo/illustration of someone using hand gestures to interact with a screen (natural). Arrow between them labeled "The shift."
    34вҶ’
    35вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The core challenge is not just recognizing gestures from one person, but building a system that works for anyone who sits in front of the webcam. That generalization requirement drives every design decision in this project."
    36вҶ’
    37вҶ’---
    38вҶ’
    39вҶ’## Slide 3: Project Objectives
    40вҶ’
    41вҶ’**Title:** Three Methods, One Question: What Works Best?
    42вҶ’
    43вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    44вҶ’- Objective 1: Implement and compare three gesture classification methods of increasing complexity
    45вҶ’  - Rule-based heuristic (no learning)
    46вҶ’  - Classical ML: Random Forest (engineered features)
    47вҶ’  - Deep Learning: MLP neural network (raw landmarks)
    48вҶ’- Objective 2: Evaluate all methods using person-aware cross-validation on a large-scale public dataset
    49вҶ’- Objective 3: Deploy the best model in a real-time browser-based puzzle game
    50вҶ’
    51вҶ’**Visual:** Three columns showing method progression: Heuristic (hand-drawn rules icon) --> Random Forest (tree diagram icon) --> MLP (neural network icon). Arrow beneath labeled "Increasing complexity."
    52вҶ’
    53вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "We deliberately chose three methods at different points on the complexity spectrum. The heuristic uses zero training data. The Random Forest learns from engineered features. The MLP learns directly from raw coordinates. This lets us measure exactly what data-driven learning buys you."
    54вҶ’
    55вҶ’---
    56вҶ’
    57вҶ’## Slide 4: Gesture Classes and Game Mapping
    58вҶ’
    59вҶ’**Title:** Five Gestures, Five Game Actions
    60вҶ’
    61вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    62вҶ’- Open Hand (spread fingers, palm forward) --> Release puzzle piece
    63вҶ’- Fist (closed hand) --> Rotate puzzle piece
    64вҶ’- Pinch (thumb + index touching) --> Grab / release piece
    65вҶ’- Frame (two hands forming rectangle) --> Take screenshot
    66вҶ’- None (no specific gesture) --> No action
    67вҶ’
    68вҶ’**Visual:** A 5-column strip. Each column shows: gesture photo/illustration on top, gesture name in the middle, game action icon on the bottom. Connected by arrows.
    69вҶ’
    70вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "These five gestures were chosen because they are visually distinct and map to natural puzzle interactions. Frame is the hardest because it requires two hands, and None is the hardest to learn because it covers everything that is not one of the other four."
    71вҶ’
    72вҶ’---
    73вҶ’
    74вҶ’## Slide 5: System Pipeline Overview
    75вҶ’
    76вҶ’**Title:** End-to-End Pipeline: From Webcam to Game Control
    77вҶ’
    78вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    79вҶ’- Stage 1: Video frame captured from webcam
    80вҶ’- Stage 2: Google MediaPipe HandLandmarker extracts 21 3D landmarks per hand
    81вҶ’- Stage 3: Preprocessing normalizes landmarks (position + scale invariant)
    82вҶ’- Stage 4: Classifier predicts one of five gesture classes
    83вҶ’- Stage 5: Prediction drives game action in browser
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’**Visual:** Reproduce the TikZ pipeline diagram from the report (Figure 5). Flow: Video Frame --> MediaPipe HandLandmarker --> 21 Landmarks (x,y,z) --> [Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP] --> Gesture Classification --> [Puzzle Game | Evaluation].
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The key architectural insight is that MediaPipe does the heavy lifting of hand detection and landmark extraction. Our classifiers only need to interpret 21 points in 3D space. This keeps the classification fast and lets us compare methods fairly on the same input representation."
    88вҶ’
    89вҶ’**Equation to show:**
    90вҶ’```
    91вҶ’Video Frame --[MediaPipe]--> L in R^{21x3} --[Classifier]--> y in {1,...,5}
    92вҶ’```
    93вҶ’
    94вҶ’---
    95вҶ’
    96вҶ’## Slide 6: Dataset -- HaGRID v2
    97вҶ’
    98вҶ’**Title:** Training Data: HaGRID v2 Dataset
    99вҶ’
   100вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   101вҶ’- HaGRID v2: 1,086,158 FullHD images, 37,583 unique subjects, 33 gesture classes
   102вҶ’- We selected 5 classes mapped to our gestures: stop-->open_hand, fist-->fist, thumb_index-->pinch, take_picture-->frame, no_gesture-->none
   103вҶ’- Extracted subset: 9,231 samples from 3,607 unique subjects
   104вҶ’- Capped at 1,000 samples per gesture per split for computational efficiency
   105вҶ’- Pre-annotated with MediaPipe landmarks (x, y only; z set to 0.0)
   106вҶ’
   107вҶ’**Visual:** Table showing the dataset split statistics:
   108вҶ’
   109вҶ’| Class     | Train | Val   | Total |
   110вҶ’|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
   111вҶ’| open_hand | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   112вҶ’| fist      | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   113вҶ’| pinch     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   114вҶ’| frame     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   115вҶ’| none      | 1,000 | 231   | 1,231 |
   116вҶ’| **Total** | **5,000** | **4,231** | **9,231** |
   117вҶ’
   118вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "HaGRID is one of the largest public hand gesture datasets available. By using pre-annotated landmarks from the dataset authors, we ensure consistency. The slight class imbalance in 'none' reflects that fewer no-gesture samples were available in the validation split."
   119вҶ’
   120вҶ’---
   121вҶ’
   122вҶ’## Slide 7: Preprocessing Pipeline
   123вҶ’
   124вҶ’**Title:** Making Landmarks Position- and Scale-Invariant
   125вҶ’
   126вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   127вҶ’- Step 1: Wrist-relative translation -- subtract wrist landmark (index 0) from all 21 landmarks, centering the hand at origin
   128вҶ’- Step 2: Scale normalization -- divide all coordinates by the Euclidean distance from wrist to middle-finger MCP (landmark 9)
   129вҶ’- Output: 20 normalized landmarks x 3 coordinates = 60-dimensional feature vector
   130вҶ’- This ensures the classifier sees the same representation regardless of hand position, distance from camera, or hand size
   131вҶ’
   132вҶ’**Visual:** Diagram showing the normalization math with a hand landmark overlay:
   133вҶ’
   134вҶ’```
   135вҶ’l_hat_i = (l_i - l_0) / ||l_9 - l_0||_2,  for i = 1,...,20
   136вҶ’```
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’Show a "before" (raw landmarks scattered on image plane) and "after" (centered at origin, unit-scaled) side by side.
   139вҶ’
   140вҶ’**Worked Example (optional callout box):**
   141вҶ’- Raw: l_0 = (0.45, 0.72, 0.0), l_9 = (0.42, 0.55, 0.0)
   142вҶ’- Scale factor d = 0.1726
   143вҶ’- All landmarks divided by d after wrist subtraction
   144вҶ’
   145вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This two-step normalization is critical. Without it, the classifier would need to learn that the same gesture at different positions and scales is the same gesture. With it, we hand the classifier a canonical representation every time."
   146вҶ’
   147вҶ’---
   148вҶ’
   149вҶ’## Slide 8: Method 1 -- Rule-Based Heuristic
   150вҶ’
   151вҶ’**Title:** Method 1: Hand-Crafted Rules (and Why They Failed)
   152вҶ’
   153вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   154вҶ’- Approach: ~20 manually tuned thresholds on finger curl angles and key distances
   155вҶ’- Classification logic:
   156вҶ’  - Open Hand: all fingers extended (curl angles below threshold)
   157вҶ’  - Fist: all fingers curled (curl angles above threshold)
   158вҶ’  - Pinch: thumb-tip to index-tip distance below proximity threshold
   159вҶ’  - Frame: two hands detected with specific spatial relationship
   160вҶ’  - None: default fallback
   161вҶ’- Result: 0.99% accuracy (worse than random chance at 20%)
   162вҶ’- No training required, but no adaptability either
   163вҶ’
   164вҶ’**Visual:** Flowchart of the heuristic decision tree. Annotate with a large red "FAILED" stamp or red X. Show the confusion matrix (heuristic_confusion_matrix.png) inset, demonstrating near-random predictions.
   165вҶ’
   166вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The heuristic scored below random chance. This happened because the rules were designed assuming 3D landmarks, but HaGRID only provides 2D coordinates with z=0. The curl angle calculations collapse without depth information. This is the strongest argument for data-driven methods: rules that seem reasonable can fail catastrophically when assumptions about the data pipeline are wrong."
   167вҶ’
   168вҶ’---
   169вҶ’
   170вҶ’## Slide 9: Method 2 -- Random Forest
   171вҶ’
   172вҶ’**Title:** Method 2: Random Forest on Engineered Distance Features
   173вҶ’
   174вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   175вҶ’- 100 decision trees, max_depth=15 (scikit-learn)
   176вҶ’- Input: 24-dimensional feature vector of pairwise Euclidean distances between key landmark pairs (fingertips, MCP joints, PIP joints)
   177вҶ’- Semi-automatic feature engineering: distances selected to capture discriminative geometric relationships
   178вҶ’- Model size: 8.6 MB (~100K tree nodes)
   179вҶ’- Result: 94.13% accuracy (+/- 1.25%), 13.2 ms latency
   180вҶ’
   181вҶ’**Visual:** Two-part visual. Left: diagram of a hand with lines drawn between the key landmark pairs used as features. Right: RF feature importance bar chart (rf_feature_importance.png) showing the top 15 most important pairwise distance features.
   182вҶ’
   183вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The Random Forest jumps from 1% to 94% accuracy by learning from data instead of guessing thresholds. The 24 pairwise distance features capture the geometric relationships that matter. Notice in the feature importance plot which finger distances are most discriminative -- this gives us interpretability that the MLP lacks."
   184вҶ’
   185вҶ’---
   186вҶ’
   187вҶ’## Slide 10: Method 3 -- MLP Neural Network
   188вҶ’
   189вҶ’**Title:** Method 3: MLP Neural Network on Raw Landmarks
   190вҶ’
   191вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   192вҶ’- Architecture: Input(60) --> Dense(128, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(64, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(5, Softmax)
   193вҶ’- Total parameters: 14,789 (model size: 227 KB -- 38x smaller than Random Forest)
   194вҶ’- No manual feature engineering: learns directly from the 60-dimensional normalized landmark vector
   195вҶ’- Training: Adam optimizer (lr=0.001), sparse categorical cross-entropy, early stopping (patience=10)
   196вҶ’- Converged in 17/50 epochs. Train acc: 98.56%, Val acc: 97.98%
   197вҶ’- Result: 98.41% accuracy (+/- 0.26%), 43.7 ms latency
   198вҶ’
   199вҶ’**Visual:** Neural network architecture diagram showing the layer dimensions:
   200вҶ’
   201вҶ’```
   202вҶ’[60 inputs] --> [128 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [64 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [5 classes + Softmax]
   203вҶ’```
   204вҶ’
   205вҶ’Annotate with parameter counts at each layer. Optionally show MLP training curve (train/val accuracy over 17 epochs).
   206вҶ’
   207вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The MLP is the simplest possible neural network -- just two hidden layers -- yet it beats the Random Forest by 4.3 percentage points. It achieves this by operating on the full 60-dimensional landmark vector rather than a hand-picked 24-dimensional distance subset. The network discovers its own features. Notice the model is only 227 KB, making it easy to deploy in a browser."
   208вҶ’
   209вҶ’---
   210вҶ’
   211вҶ’## Slide 11: Methods Comparison Table
   212вҶ’
   213вҶ’**Title:** Three Methods Side-by-Side
   214вҶ’
   215вҶ’**Key Bullets (as table):**
   216вҶ’
   217вҶ’| Aspect              | Heuristic       | Random Forest   | MLP             |
   218вҶ’|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
   219вҶ’| Input Features      | Angles + dist.  | 24 pairwise dist.| 60 raw coords  |
   220вҶ’| Feature Engineering | Manual          | Semi-automatic  | None (end-to-end)|
   221вҶ’| Training Required   | No              | Yes (fast)      | Yes (moderate)  |
   222вҶ’| Parameters          | ~20 thresholds  | ~100K nodes     | 14,789 weights  |
   223вҶ’| Model Size          | 170 B           | 8.6 MB          | 227 KB          |
   224вҶ’| Interpretability    | High            | Medium          | Low             |
   225вҶ’| CV Accuracy         | 0.99%           | 94.13%          | 98.41%          |
   226вҶ’| Latency             | 0.1 ms          | 13.2 ms         | 43.7 ms         |
   227вҶ’
   228вҶ’**Visual:** Clean comparison table. Highlight the MLP row/column in green for accuracy, the Heuristic in green for speed, the RF as the middle ground. Use color coding to make trade-offs immediately visible.
   229вҶ’
   230вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This table is the core deliverable of the project. Three takeaways: First, data-driven methods crush hand-crafted rules. Second, the MLP is 38x smaller than the Random Forest yet 4 points more accurate. Third, all three methods have latencies well under 1 second, but only the heuristic and RF fit within a 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms."
   231вҶ’
   232вҶ’---
   233вҶ’
   234вҶ’## Slide 12: Evaluation Methodology
   235вҶ’
   236вҶ’**Title:** Person-Aware Cross-Validation: Testing on Strangers
   237вҶ’
   238вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   239вҶ’- Group 5-Fold Cross-Validation using person IDs as groups (sklearn GroupKFold)
   240вҶ’- Guarantee: No subject appears in both training and test sets within any fold
   241вҶ’- This tests generalization to truly unseen individuals, not just unseen images of the same person
   242вҶ’- 3,607 unique subjects ensure diverse hand shapes, skin tones, and gesture styles
   243вҶ’- Full Leave-One-Person-Out CV was computationally impractical at this scale
   244вҶ’- Secondary metric: stratified 80/20 split for comparison
   245вҶ’
   246вҶ’**Visual:** Diagram showing 5 folds with person silhouette icons. Each fold clearly separates "train persons" from "test persons" with no overlap. Annotate: "Person A never appears in both train and test within the same fold."
   247вҶ’
   248вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This is the most important methodological choice in the project. Standard random splitting would leak person identity into the test set -- the model could memorize a specific person's hand shape rather than learning gesture geometry. Group K-Fold eliminates that risk. Our results reflect how well the system works on someone it has never seen before."
   249вҶ’
   250вҶ’---
   251вҶ’
   252вҶ’## Slide 13: Results -- Accuracy vs. Latency Tradeoff
   253вҶ’
   254вҶ’**Title:** The Accuracy-Latency Tradeoff
   255вҶ’
   256вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   257вҶ’- Heuristic: 0.99% accuracy at 0.1 ms (fast but useless)
   258вҶ’- Random Forest: 94.13% at 13.2 ms (fits within 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms)
   259вҶ’- MLP: 98.41% at 43.7 ms (highest accuracy, exceeds 60 FPS budget but acceptable for game control at ~23 FPS)
   260вҶ’- For puzzle game control, 23 FPS is sufficient -- gesture changes happen at human speed (~1-2 per second)
   261вҶ’- The 60 FPS line matters for frame-by-frame rendering, not gesture classification
   262вҶ’
   263вҶ’**Visual:** Reproduce the scatter plot from the report (Figure 1): X-axis = Inference Latency (ms), Y-axis = CV Accuracy (%). Three labeled dots (Heuristic, RF, MLP). Dashed red vertical line at 16.7 ms labeled "60 FPS budget."
   264вҶ’
   265вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This chart tells the full story. The heuristic is in the bottom-left corner -- fast but broken. The Random Forest sits just inside the 60 FPS budget. The MLP is the clear accuracy winner but crosses the 60 FPS line. For our puzzle game, this is fine. You do not need 60 gesture predictions per second. You need accurate ones."
   266вҶ’
   267вҶ’---
   268вҶ’
   269вҶ’## Slide 14: Confusion Matrices -- RF vs MLP
   270вҶ’
   271вҶ’**Title:** Where Do the Models Get Confused?
   272вҶ’
   273вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   274вҶ’- Random Forest struggles most with:
   275вҶ’  - Frame gesture (F1 = 0.892): two-hand gesture loses spatial context when landmarks are per-hand
   276вҶ’  - Pinch gesture (F1 = 0.919): subtle finger proximity differences
   277вҶ’- MLP handles all classes above 95% F1:
   278вҶ’  - None class (F1 = 0.953): inherently diverse (any non-gesture position)
   279вҶ’  - Frame (F1 = 0.973): same two-hand limitation but MLP compensates with richer features
   280вҶ’- MLP reduces frame confusion by 8.1 percentage points over RF
   281вҶ’
   282вҶ’**Visual:** Side-by-side confusion matrices: Left = rf_confusion_matrix.png, Right = mlp_confusion_matrix.png. Annotate the key confusion cells (frame-pinch confusion in RF, none-class errors in MLP).
   283вҶ’
   284вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Look at the frame row in the Random Forest matrix -- significant confusion with pinch and none. The MLP cleans this up dramatically. The remaining errors in the MLP are concentrated in the 'none' class, which makes sense: 'none' is a catch-all category that includes every possible non-gesture hand position."
   285вҶ’
   286вҶ’---
   287вҶ’
   288вҶ’## Slide 15: Per-Class F1 Score Comparison
   289вҶ’
   290вҶ’**Title:** Per-Class F1: MLP Dominates Every Category
   291вҶ’
   292вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   293вҶ’- MLP exceeds RF on all five gesture classes
   294вҶ’- Biggest MLP advantage: Frame (+8.1 pp) and Pinch (+7.6 pp)
   295вҶ’- Open Hand and Fist are "easy" gestures for both methods (F1 > 0.98)
   296вҶ’- The hard gestures (frame, pinch, none) are where model sophistication pays off
   297вҶ’
   298вҶ’**Breakdown:**
   299вҶ’
   300вҶ’| Class     | RF F1  | MLP F1 | Delta   |
   301вҶ’|-----------|--------|--------|---------|
   302вҶ’| open_hand | 0.985  | 0.994  | +0.009  |
   303вҶ’| fist      | 0.983  | 0.993  | +0.010  |
   304вҶ’| pinch     | 0.919  | 0.995  | +0.076  |
   305вҶ’| frame     | 0.892  | 0.973  | +0.081  |
   306вҶ’| none      | 0.919  | 0.953  | +0.034  |
   307вҶ’
   308вҶ’**Visual:** Grouped bar chart (per_class_f1_comparison.png) with RF and MLP bars side by side for each gesture class. Annotate the delta values above the bars.
   309вҶ’
   310вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The easy gestures -- open hand and fist -- are already near-perfect for both methods. The real differentiation happens on the hard gestures. The MLP's ability to learn from the full 60-dimensional landmark vector gives it an edge on the subtle distinctions that hand-engineered distance features miss."
   311вҶ’
   312вҶ’---
   313вҶ’
   314вҶ’## Slide 16: Cross-Validation Stability
   315вҶ’
   316вҶ’**Title:** Fold Stability: MLP Generalizes Consistently
   317вҶ’
   318вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   319вҶ’- MLP: std = 0.26% across 5 folds (range: 97.89% to 98.59%)
   320вҶ’- RF: std = 1.25% across 5 folds (range: 92.09% to 95.72%)
   321вҶ’- MLP 95% CI: [98.16%, 98.64%] -- tight bounds, high confidence
   322вҶ’- RF fold 4 dip (92.09%) suggests sensitivity to specific person groups
   323вҶ’- MLP is robust to person variation -- critical for real-world deployment
   324вҶ’
   325вҶ’**Visual:** Table of fold-by-fold accuracies (from Table 6 in the report), plus a line chart showing MLP as a nearly flat line and RF as a more volatile line across 5 folds.
   326вҶ’
   327вҶ’| Fold | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP    |
   328вҶ’|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
   329вҶ’| 1    | 0.60%     | 93.45%        | 98.48% |
   330вҶ’| 2    | 0.98%     | 94.64%        | 97.89% |
   331вҶ’| 3    | 1.30%     | 94.75%        | 98.54% |
   332вҶ’| 4    | 1.25%     | 92.09%        | 98.54% |
   333вҶ’| 5    | 0.81%     | 95.72%        | 98.59% |
   334вҶ’
   335вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Accuracy on one test split can be misleading. What matters is consistency. The MLP varies by less than 0.7 percentage points across all five folds, meaning it works equally well regardless of which group of people ends up in the test set. The Random Forest dips to 92% on fold 4, suggesting some person groups have hand geometries that the 24-distance feature set does not capture well."
   336вҶ’
   337вҶ’---
   338вҶ’
   339вҶ’## Slide 17: Live Demo / Game Demo
   340вҶ’
   341вҶ’**Title:** Live Demo: Puzzle Game Controlled by Hand Gestures
   342вҶ’
   343вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   344вҶ’- Browser-based jigsaw puzzle game (HTML/CSS/JavaScript)
   345вҶ’- Real-time webcam feed processed by MediaPipe in-browser
   346вҶ’- MLP model loaded via TensorFlow.js for client-side inference
   347вҶ’- Gesture controls: Open hand (release), Fist (rotate), Pinch (grab), Frame (screenshot)
   348вҶ’- Fully client-side: no server required, works offline after loading
   349вҶ’
   350вҶ’**Demo flow:**
   351вҶ’1. Show the webcam feed with landmark overlay
   352вҶ’2. Demonstrate each gesture and its game action
   353вҶ’3. Complete a simple puzzle move (grab a piece, move it, release it)
   354вҶ’
   355вҶ’**Visual:** Screenshot of the game interface showing the puzzle on one side and the webcam feed with hand landmarks on the other. If live demo is not possible, show a screen recording or sequence of screenshots.
   356вҶ’
   357вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Let me show you the system in action. Everything runs in the browser -- MediaPipe detects my hand, extracts landmarks, the MLP classifies the gesture, and the game responds. Watch how I use pinch to grab a piece, move my hand to reposition it, then open my hand to release. The latency is imperceptible at human interaction speed."
   358вҶ’
   359вҶ’---
   360вҶ’
   361вҶ’## Slide 18: Software Engineering and Testing
   362вҶ’
   363вҶ’**Title:** Engineering Quality: 28 Tests, Modular Architecture
   364вҶ’
   365вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   366вҶ’- 28 automated tests covering preprocessing, feature extraction, all three classifiers, evaluation pipeline, and data conversion
   367вҶ’- Modular codebase: each method is a separate module with clean interfaces
   368вҶ’  - `preprocessing.py` -- shared normalization pipeline
   369вҶ’  - `features.py` -- feature extraction (angles, distances)
   370вҶ’  - `heuristic.py`, `random_forest.py`, `mlp.py` -- one file per method
   371вҶ’  - `evaluate.py` -- unified evaluation with CV, metrics, and plots
   372вҶ’  - `train.py` -- training orchestrator
   373вҶ’- Reproducible: single command runs full pipeline from data extraction to evaluation
   374вҶ’- All plots and metrics generated automatically
   375вҶ’
   376вҶ’**Visual:** Project structure tree (from Section 5.2 of report), with test coverage callout. Optionally show a terminal screenshot of `pytest` passing all 28 tests.
   377вҶ’
   378вҶ’```
   379вҶ’CVsubject/
   380вҶ’|-- ml/
   381вҶ’|   |-- preprocessing.py
   382вҶ’|   |-- features.py
   383вҶ’|   |-- heuristic.py
   384вҶ’|   |-- random_forest.py
   385вҶ’|   |-- mlp.py
   386вҶ’|   |-- evaluate.py
   387вҶ’|   |-- train.py
   388вҶ’|   |-- tests/test_core.py  (28 tests)
   389вҶ’|-- game/
   390вҶ’|   |-- index.html, game.js, gesture.js, main.js
   391вҶ’|-- models/
   392вҶ’|   |-- plots/ (6 auto-generated charts)
   393вҶ’```
   394вҶ’
   395вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This is not just a Jupyter notebook experiment. The codebase is modular, tested, and reproducible. Every module has a single responsibility. The 28 tests ensure that preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification behave correctly even as we iterate on the models. You can reproduce every result in this presentation with one command."
   396вҶ’
   397вҶ’---
   398вҶ’
   399вҶ’## Slide 19: Limitations and Future Work
   400вҶ’
   401вҶ’**Title:** What We Would Do Next
   402вҶ’
   403вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   404вҶ’- Current Limitations:
   405вҶ’  - Heuristic needs recalibration for 2D landmarks (or re-extract with full 3D using MediaPipe Python API on raw images)
   406вҶ’  - Frame gesture is inherently limited by per-hand landmark extraction -- a two-stage frame detector analyzing spatial relationship between both hands would improve this
   407вҶ’  - MLP exceeds 60 FPS budget (43.7 ms) -- acceptable for games, not for real-time rendering pipelines
   408вҶ’- Future Work:
   409вҶ’  - Model quantization: convert MLP to TFLite or ONNX for faster browser inference via WebAssembly
   410вҶ’  - User adaptation: fine-tune on specific user's hand geometry for personalized accuracy
   411вҶ’  - Temporal modeling: LSTM or 1D-CNN over landmark frame sequences for motion-aware gesture recognition (e.g., distinguishing a wave from an open hand)
   412вҶ’  - Expanded gesture vocabulary beyond 5 classes
   413вҶ’
   414вҶ’**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left column: "Limitations" with warning icons. Right column: "Future Work" with forward-arrow icons. Each item is a short phrase, not a sentence.
   415вҶ’
   416вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Every system has limits. Our biggest limitation is the frame gesture -- we extract landmarks per hand, so the two-hand spatial relationship is lost. The most impactful future work would be temporal modeling. Right now we classify single frames independently. A sequence model could distinguish dynamic gestures like waving from static ones like open hand, and would also smooth out frame-to-frame noise."
   417вҶ’
   418вҶ’---
   419вҶ’
   420вҶ’## Slide 20: Conclusion and Key Takeaway
   421вҶ’
   422вҶ’**Title:** One Slide Summary
   423вҶ’
   424вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   425вҶ’- The MLP neural network is the clear winner: 98.41% accuracy, 0.26% standard deviation, all classes above 95% F1
   426вҶ’- Random Forest is the best practical tradeoff: 94.13% accuracy within the 60 FPS latency budget
   427вҶ’- The heuristic's failure (0.99%) is itself a finding: hand-crafted rules are brittle to data pipeline assumptions
   428вҶ’- Person-aware evaluation (Group K-Fold) ensures results reflect real-world generalization
   429вҶ’- The system works end-to-end: from webcam to browser-based puzzle game control
   430вҶ’
   431вҶ’**Key Takeaway (large, centered text):**
   432вҶ’> "Data-driven methods do not just outperform heuristics -- they are 100x more accurate. But the gap between classical ML and deep learning is only 4 percentage points. Choose based on your latency budget."
   433вҶ’
   434вҶ’**Visual:** Summary table (from Table 8 in report):
   435вҶ’
   436вҶ’| Criterion               | Best Method   | Score          |
   437вҶ’|--------------------------|---------------|----------------|
   438вҶ’| Overall Accuracy         | MLP           | 98.41%         |
   439вҶ’| Inference Speed          | Heuristic     | 0.1 ms         |
   440вҶ’| Accuracy/Latency Balance | Random Forest | 94.13% @ 13 ms |
   441вҶ’| Per-Class Consistency    | MLP           | All > 95% F1   |
   442вҶ’| Model Size               | Heuristic     | 170 B          |
   443вҶ’| Generalization (low std) | MLP           | +/- 0.26%      |
   444вҶ’
   445вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "If you remember one thing from this presentation, let it be this: the jump from heuristic to data-driven is enormous -- from 1% to 94%. But the jump from classical ML to deep learning is incremental -- from 94% to 98%. That second jump costs you 3x the latency. For most real-time applications, either the Random Forest or the MLP will serve you well. The heuristic is a cautionary tale about assumptions."
   446вҶ’
   447вҶ’---
   448вҶ’
   449вҶ’## Appendix: Presentation Timing Guide
   450вҶ’
   451вҶ’| Slide(s) | Section                      | Time    | Cumulative |
   452вҶ’|-----------|------------------------------|---------|------------|
   453вҶ’| 1         | Title                        | 0:30    | 0:30       |
   454вҶ’| 2-3       | Problem + Objectives         | 1:30    | 2:00       |
   455вҶ’| 4         | Gesture Classes              | 1:00    | 3:00       |
   456вҶ’| 5         | Pipeline Overview            | 1:30    | 4:30       |
   457вҶ’| 6-7       | Dataset + Preprocessing      | 2:00    | 6:30       |
   458вҶ’| 8-10      | Three Methods (deep dive)    | 3:30    | 10:00      |
   459вҶ’| 11        | Methods Comparison Table     | 1:00    | 11:00      |
   460вҶ’| 12        | Evaluation Methodology       | 1:00    | 12:00      |
   461вҶ’| 13-16     | Results + Analysis           | 3:00    | 15:00      |
   462вҶ’| 17        | Live Demo                    | 2:00    | 17:00      |
   463вҶ’| 18        | Testing + Engineering        | 0:30    | 17:30      |
   464вҶ’| 19        | Limitations + Future Work    | 1:00    | 18:30      |
   465вҶ’| 20        | Conclusion                   | 1:00    | 19:30      |
   466вҶ’|           | Q&A Buffer                   | 0:30    | 20:00      |
   467вҶ’
   468вҶ’---
   469вҶ’
   470вҶ’## Appendix: Visual Assets Checklist
   471вҶ’
   472вҶ’All plots are pre-generated in `/models/plots/`:
   473вҶ’
   474вҶ’| Slide | Visual Needed                           | Source File                                    |
   475вҶ’|-------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
   476вҶ’| 8     | Heuristic confusion matrix              | `models/plots/heuristic_confusion_matrix.png`  |
   477вҶ’| 9     | RF feature importance                   | `models/plots/rf_feature_importance.png`       |
   478вҶ’| 13    | Accuracy vs latency scatter             | Recreate from report Figure 1 (TikZ)          |
   479вҶ’| 14    | RF confusion matrix                     | `models/plots/rf_confusion_matrix.png`         |
   480вҶ’| 14    | MLP confusion matrix                    | `models/plots/mlp_confusion_matrix.png`        |
   481вҶ’| 15    | Per-class F1 comparison                 | `models/plots/per_class_f1_comparison.png`     |
   482вҶ’| --    | Overall accuracy comparison             | `models/plots/accuracy_comparison.png`         |
   483вҶ’
   484вҶ’**Additional visuals to create:**
   485вҶ’- Slide 4: Gesture class photo strip (source from HaGRID samples or take new photos)
   486вҶ’- Slide 5: Pipeline diagram (adapt TikZ from report or recreate in slide tool)
   487вҶ’- Slide 7: Before/after normalization diagram
   488вҶ’- Slide 10: MLP architecture diagram
   489вҶ’- Slide 12: Group K-Fold person diagram
   490вҶ’- Slide 17: Game screenshot or screen recording
   491вҶ’
   492вҶ’---
   493вҶ’
   494вҶ’## Appendix: Design Recommendations
   495вҶ’
   496вҶ’**Color Palette:**
   497вҶ’- Primary: Deep blue (#003399) for headings and key data
   498вҶ’- Accent: Green (#2E8B57) for MLP results / positive outcomes
   499вҶ’- Warning: Red (#CC3333) for heuristic failure / limitations
   500вҶ’- Neutral: Gray (#666666) for secondary text and RF results
   501вҶ’
   502вҶ’**Typography:**
   503вҶ’- Slide titles: Bold, 28-32pt
   504вҶ’- Body bullets: Regular, 20-24pt
   505вҶ’- Table text: Regular, 16-18pt
   506вҶ’- Speaker notes: Not shown on slides
   507вҶ’
   508вҶ’**General Rules:**
   509вҶ’- Maximum 5 bullet points per slide
   510вҶ’- One key message per slide
   511вҶ’- Every slide has a visual element -- no text-only slides
   512вҶ’- Numbers are highlighted in bold or accent color
   513вҶ’- Use progressive reveal (animation) sparingly -- only for the methods comparison table and results
   514вҶ’
