1вҶ’# Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
     2вҶ’## Presentation Outline (15-20 minutes, 18 slides)
     3вҶ’
     4вҶ’---
     5вҶ’
     6вҶ’## Slide 1: Title Slide
     7вҶ’
     8вҶ’**Title:** Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
     9вҶ’**Subtitle:** A Comparative Study of Heuristic, Classical ML, and Deep Learning Approaches
    10вҶ’
    11вҶ’**Key Elements:**
    12вҶ’- Course: Computer Vision
    13вҶ’- Student Name / Student ID
    14вҶ’- Date: February 2026
    15вҶ’- University logo (if applicable)
    16вҶ’
    17вҶ’**Visual:** Clean title layout with a composite image showing the five gesture classes (open hand, fist, pinch, frame, none) as small icons or photos across the bottom.
    18вҶ’
    19вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Today I will present a hand gesture recognition system that lets you control a browser-based jigsaw puzzle using just your webcam. We compare three classification methods of increasing complexity and show why data-driven approaches dominate hand-crafted rules."
    20вҶ’
    21вҶ’---
    22вҶ’
    23вҶ’## Slide 2: Problem Statement and Motivation
    24вҶ’
    25вҶ’**Title:** Why Hand Gesture Recognition?
    26вҶ’
    27вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    28вҶ’- Human-computer interaction is moving beyond keyboards and mice toward natural, touchless interfaces
    29вҶ’- Hand gestures are intuitive, require no hardware beyond a webcam, and map naturally to spatial tasks like puzzle manipulation
    30вҶ’- Challenge: Gestures vary dramatically across individuals (hand size, skin tone, angle, lighting)
    31вҶ’- Our goal: Build a real-time gesture classifier that generalizes to unseen users
    32вҶ’
    33вҶ’**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left: photo of someone using a keyboard (traditional). Right: photo/illustration of someone using hand gestures to interact with a screen (natural). Arrow between them labeled "The shift."
    34вҶ’
    35вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The core challenge is not just recognizing gestures from one person, but building a system that works for anyone who sits in front of the webcam. That generalization requirement drives every design decision in this project."
    36вҶ’
    37вҶ’---
    38вҶ’
    39вҶ’## Slide 3: Project Objectives
    40вҶ’
    41вҶ’**Title:** Three Methods, One Question: What Works Best?
    42вҶ’
    43вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    44вҶ’- Objective 1: Implement and compare three gesture classification methods of increasing complexity
    45вҶ’  - Rule-based heuristic (no learning)
    46вҶ’  - Classical ML: Random Forest (engineered features)
    47вҶ’  - Deep Learning: MLP neural network (raw landmarks)
    48вҶ’- Objective 2: Evaluate all methods using person-aware cross-validation on a large-scale public dataset
    49вҶ’- Objective 3: Deploy the best model in a real-time browser-based puzzle game
    50вҶ’
    51вҶ’**Visual:** Three columns showing method progression: Heuristic (hand-drawn rules icon) --> Random Forest (tree diagram icon) --> MLP (neural network icon). Arrow beneath labeled "Increasing complexity."
    52вҶ’
    53вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "We deliberately chose three methods at different points on the complexity spectrum. The heuristic uses zero training data. The Random Forest learns from engineered features. The MLP learns directly from raw coordinates. This lets us measure exactly what data-driven learning buys you."
    54вҶ’
    55вҶ’---
    56вҶ’
    57вҶ’## Slide 4: Gesture Classes and Game Mapping
    58вҶ’
    59вҶ’**Title:** Five Gestures, Five Game Actions
    60вҶ’
    61вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    62вҶ’- Open Hand (spread fingers, palm forward) --> Release puzzle piece
    63вҶ’- Fist (closed hand) --> Rotate puzzle piece
    64вҶ’- Pinch (thumb + index touching) --> Grab / release piece
    65вҶ’- Frame (two hands forming rectangle) --> Take screenshot
    66вҶ’- None (no specific gesture) --> No action
    67вҶ’
    68вҶ’**Visual:** A 5-column strip. Each column shows: gesture photo/illustration on top, gesture name in the middle, game action icon on the bottom. Connected by arrows.
    69вҶ’
    70вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "These five gestures were chosen because they are visually distinct and map to natural puzzle interactions. Frame is the hardest because it requires two hands, and None is the hardest to learn because it covers everything that is not one of the other four."
    71вҶ’
    72вҶ’---
    73вҶ’
    74вҶ’## Slide 5: System Pipeline Overview
    75вҶ’
    76вҶ’**Title:** End-to-End Pipeline: From Webcam to Game Control
    77вҶ’
    78вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
    79вҶ’- Stage 1: Video frame captured from webcam
    80вҶ’- Stage 2: Google MediaPipe HandLandmarker extracts 21 3D landmarks per hand
    81вҶ’- Stage 3: Preprocessing normalizes landmarks (position + scale invariant)
    82вҶ’- Stage 4: Classifier predicts one of five gesture classes
    83вҶ’- Stage 5: Prediction drives game action in browser
    84вҶ’
    85вҶ’**Visual:** Reproduce the TikZ pipeline diagram from the report (Figure 5). Flow: Video Frame --> MediaPipe HandLandmarker --> 21 Landmarks (x,y,z) --> [Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP] --> Gesture Classification --> [Puzzle Game | Evaluation].
    86вҶ’
    87вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The key architectural insight is that MediaPipe does the heavy lifting of hand detection and landmark extraction. Our classifiers only need to interpret 21 points in 3D space. This keeps the classification fast and lets us compare methods fairly on the same input representation."
    88вҶ’
    89вҶ’**Equation to show:**
    90вҶ’```
    91вҶ’Video Frame --[MediaPipe]--> L in R^{21x3} --[Classifier]--> y in {1,...,5}
    92вҶ’```
    93вҶ’
    94вҶ’---
    95вҶ’
    96вҶ’## Slide 6: Dataset -- HaGRID v2
    97вҶ’
    98вҶ’**Title:** Training Data: HaGRID v2 Dataset
    99вҶ’
   100вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   101вҶ’- HaGRID v2: 1,086,158 FullHD images, 37,583 unique subjects, 33 gesture classes
   102вҶ’- We selected 5 classes mapped to our gestures: stop-->open_hand, fist-->fist, thumb_index-->pinch, take_picture-->frame, no_gesture-->none
   103вҶ’- Extracted subset: 9,231 samples from 3,607 unique subjects
   104вҶ’- Capped at 1,000 samples per gesture per split for computational efficiency
   105вҶ’- Pre-annotated with MediaPipe landmarks (x, y only; z set to 0.0)
   106вҶ’
   107вҶ’**Visual:** Table showing the dataset split statistics:
   108вҶ’
   109вҶ’| Class     | Train | Val   | Total |
   110вҶ’|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
   111вҶ’| open_hand | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   112вҶ’| fist      | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   113вҶ’| pinch     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   114вҶ’| frame     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
   115вҶ’| none      | 1,000 | 231   | 1,231 |
   116вҶ’| **Total** | **5,000** | **4,231** | **9,231** |
   117вҶ’
   118вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "HaGRID is one of the largest public hand gesture datasets available. By using pre-annotated landmarks from the dataset authors, we ensure consistency. The slight class imbalance in 'none' reflects that fewer no-gesture samples were available in the validation split."
   119вҶ’
   120вҶ’---
   121вҶ’
   122вҶ’## Slide 7: Preprocessing Pipeline
   123вҶ’
   124вҶ’**Title:** Making Landmarks Position- and Scale-Invariant
   125вҶ’
   126вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   127вҶ’- Step 1: Wrist-relative translation -- subtract wrist landmark (index 0) from all 21 landmarks, centering the hand at origin
   128вҶ’- Step 2: Scale normalization -- divide all coordinates by the Euclidean distance from wrist to middle-finger MCP (landmark 9)
   129вҶ’- Output: 20 normalized landmarks x 3 coordinates = 60-dimensional feature vector
   130вҶ’- This ensures the classifier sees the same representation regardless of hand position, distance from camera, or hand size
   131вҶ’
   132вҶ’**Visual:** Diagram showing the normalization math with a hand landmark overlay:
   133вҶ’
   134вҶ’```
   135вҶ’l_hat_i = (l_i - l_0) / ||l_9 - l_0||_2,  for i = 1,...,20
   136вҶ’```
   137вҶ’
   138вҶ’Show a "before" (raw landmarks scattered on image plane) and "after" (centered at origin, unit-scaled) side by side.
   139вҶ’
   140вҶ’**Worked Example (optional callout box):**
   141вҶ’- Raw: l_0 = (0.45, 0.72, 0.0), l_9 = (0.42, 0.55, 0.0)
   142вҶ’- Scale factor d = 0.1726
   143вҶ’- All landmarks divided by d after wrist subtraction
   144вҶ’
   145вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This two-step normalization is critical. Without it, the classifier would need to learn that the same gesture at different positions and scales is the same gesture. With it, we hand the classifier a canonical representation every time."
   146вҶ’
   147вҶ’---
   148вҶ’
   149вҶ’## Slide 8: Method 1 -- Rule-Based Heuristic
   150вҶ’
   151вҶ’**Title:** Method 1: Hand-Crafted Rules (and Why They Failed)
   152вҶ’
   153вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   154вҶ’- Approach: ~20 manually tuned thresholds on finger curl angles and key distances
   155вҶ’- Classification logic:
   156вҶ’  - Open Hand: all fingers extended (curl angles below threshold)
   157вҶ’  - Fist: all fingers curled (curl angles above threshold)
   158вҶ’  - Pinch: thumb-tip to index-tip distance below proximity threshold
   159вҶ’  - Frame: two hands detected with specific spatial relationship
   160вҶ’  - None: default fallback
   161вҶ’- Result: 0.99% accuracy (worse than random chance at 20%)
   162вҶ’- No training required, but no adaptability either
   163вҶ’
   164вҶ’**Visual:** Flowchart of the heuristic decision tree. Annotate with a large red "FAILED" stamp or red X. Show the confusion matrix (heuristic_confusion_matrix.png) inset, demonstrating near-random predictions.
   165вҶ’
   166вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The heuristic scored below random chance. This happened because the rules were designed assuming 3D landmarks, but HaGRID only provides 2D coordinates with z=0. The curl angle calculations collapse without depth information. This is the strongest argument for data-driven methods: rules that seem reasonable can fail catastrophically when assumptions about the data pipeline are wrong."
   167вҶ’
   168вҶ’---
   169вҶ’
   170вҶ’## Slide 9: Method 2 -- Random Forest
   171вҶ’
   172вҶ’**Title:** Method 2: Random Forest on Engineered Distance Features
   173вҶ’
   174вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   175вҶ’- 100 decision trees, max_depth=15 (scikit-learn)
   176вҶ’- Input: 24-dimensional feature vector of pairwise Euclidean distances between key landmark pairs (fingertips, MCP joints, PIP joints)
   177вҶ’- Semi-automatic feature engineering: distances selected to capture discriminative geometric relationships
   178вҶ’- Model size: 8.6 MB (~100K tree nodes)
   179вҶ’- Result: 94.13% accuracy (+/- 1.25%), 13.2 ms latency
   180вҶ’
   181вҶ’**Visual:** Two-part visual. Left: diagram of a hand with lines drawn between the key landmark pairs used as features. Right: RF feature importance bar chart (rf_feature_importance.png) showing the top 15 most important pairwise distance features.
   182вҶ’
   183вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The Random Forest jumps from 1% to 94% accuracy by learning from data instead of guessing thresholds. The 24 pairwise distance features capture the geometric relationships that matter. Notice in the feature importance plot which finger distances are most discriminative -- this gives us interpretability that the MLP lacks."
   184вҶ’
   185вҶ’---
   186вҶ’
   187вҶ’## Slide 10: Method 3 -- MLP Neural Network
   188вҶ’
   189вҶ’**Title:** Method 3: MLP Neural Network on Raw Landmarks
   190вҶ’
   191вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   192вҶ’- Architecture: Input(60) --> Dense(128, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(64, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(5, Softmax)
   193вҶ’- Total parameters: 14,789 (model size: 227 KB -- 38x smaller than Random Forest)
   194вҶ’- No manual feature engineering: learns directly from the 60-dimensional normalized landmark vector
   195вҶ’- Training: Adam optimizer (lr=0.001), sparse categorical cross-entropy, early stopping (patience=10)
   196вҶ’- Converged in 17/50 epochs. Train acc: 98.56%, Val acc: 97.98%
   197вҶ’- Result: 98.41% accuracy (+/- 0.26%), 43.7 ms latency
   198вҶ’
   199вҶ’**Visual:** Neural network architecture diagram showing the layer dimensions:
   200вҶ’
   201вҶ’```
   202вҶ’[60 inputs] --> [128 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [64 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [5 classes + Softmax]
   203вҶ’```
   204вҶ’
   205вҶ’Annotate with parameter counts at each layer. Optionally show MLP training curve (train/val accuracy over 17 epochs).
   206вҶ’
   207вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The MLP is the simplest possible neural network -- just two hidden layers -- yet it beats the Random Forest by 4.3 percentage points. It achieves this by operating on the full 60-dimensional landmark vector rather than a hand-picked 24-dimensional distance subset. The network discovers its own features. Notice the model is only 227 KB, making it easy to deploy in a browser."
   208вҶ’
   209вҶ’---
   210вҶ’
   211вҶ’## Slide 11: Methods Comparison Table
   212вҶ’
   213вҶ’**Title:** Three Methods Side-by-Side
   214вҶ’
   215вҶ’**Key Bullets (as table):**
   216вҶ’
   217вҶ’| Aspect              | Heuristic       | Random Forest   | MLP             |
   218вҶ’|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
   219вҶ’| Input Features      | Angles + dist.  | 24 pairwise dist.| 60 raw coords  |
   220вҶ’| Feature Engineering | Manual          | Semi-automatic  | None (end-to-end)|
   221вҶ’| Training Required   | No              | Yes (fast)      | Yes (moderate)  |
   222вҶ’| Parameters          | ~20 thresholds  | ~100K nodes     | 14,789 weights  |
   223вҶ’| Model Size          | 170 B           | 8.6 MB          | 227 KB          |
   224вҶ’| Interpretability    | High            | Medium          | Low             |
   225вҶ’| CV Accuracy         | 0.99%           | 94.13%          | 98.41%          |
   226вҶ’| Latency             | 0.1 ms          | 13.2 ms         | 43.7 ms         |
   227вҶ’
   228вҶ’**Visual:** Clean comparison table. Highlight the MLP row/column in green for accuracy, the Heuristic in green for speed, the RF as the middle ground. Use color coding to make trade-offs immediately visible.
   229вҶ’
   230вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This table is the core deliverable of the project. Three takeaways: First, data-driven methods crush hand-crafted rules. Second, the MLP is 38x smaller than the Random Forest yet 4 points more accurate. Third, all three methods have latencies well under 1 second, but only the heuristic and RF fit within a 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms."
   231вҶ’
   232вҶ’---
   233вҶ’
   234вҶ’## Slide 12: Evaluation Methodology
   235вҶ’
   236вҶ’**Title:** Person-Aware Cross-Validation: Testing on Strangers
   237вҶ’
   238вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   239вҶ’- Group 5-Fold Cross-Validation using person IDs as groups (sklearn GroupKFold)
   240вҶ’- Guarantee: No subject appears in both training and test sets within any fold
   241вҶ’- This tests generalization to truly unseen individuals, not just unseen images of the same person
   242вҶ’- 3,607 unique subjects ensure diverse hand shapes, skin tones, and gesture styles
   243вҶ’- Full Leave-One-Person-Out CV was computationally impractical at this scale
   244вҶ’- Secondary metric: stratified 80/20 split for comparison
   245вҶ’
   246вҶ’**Visual:** Diagram showing 5 folds with person silhouette icons. Each fold clearly separates "train persons" from "test persons" with no overlap. Annotate: "Person A never appears in both train and test within the same fold."
   247вҶ’
   248вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This is the most important methodological choice in the project. Standard random splitting would leak person identity into the test set -- the model could memorize a specific person's hand shape rather than learning gesture geometry. Group K-Fold eliminates that risk. Our results reflect how well the system works on someone it has never seen before."
   249вҶ’
   250вҶ’---
   251вҶ’
   252вҶ’## Slide 13: Results -- Accuracy vs. Latency Tradeoff
   253вҶ’
   254вҶ’**Title:** The Accuracy-Latency Tradeoff
   255вҶ’
   256вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   257вҶ’- Heuristic: 0.99% accuracy at 0.1 ms (fast but useless)
   258вҶ’- Random Forest: 94.13% at 13.2 ms (fits within 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms)
   259вҶ’- MLP: 98.41% at 43.7 ms (highest accuracy, exceeds 60 FPS budget but acceptable for game control at ~23 FPS)
   260вҶ’- For puzzle game control, 23 FPS is sufficient -- gesture changes happen at human speed (~1-2 per second)
   261вҶ’- The 60 FPS line matters for frame-by-frame rendering, not gesture classification
   262вҶ’
   263вҶ’**Visual:** Reproduce the scatter plot from the report (Figure 1): X-axis = Inference Latency (ms), Y-axis = CV Accuracy (%). Three labeled dots (Heuristic, RF, MLP). Dashed red vertical line at 16.7 ms labeled "60 FPS budget."
   264вҶ’
   265вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This chart tells the full story. The heuristic is in the bottom-left corner -- fast but broken. The Random Forest sits just inside the 60 FPS budget. The MLP is the clear accuracy winner but crosses the 60 FPS line. For our puzzle game, this is fine. You do not need 60 gesture predictions per second. You need accurate ones."
   266вҶ’
   267вҶ’---
   268вҶ’
   269вҶ’## Slide 14: Confusion Matrices -- RF vs MLP
   270вҶ’
   271вҶ’**Title:** Where Do the Models Get Confused?
   272вҶ’
   273вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   274вҶ’- Random Forest struggles most with:
   275вҶ’  - Frame gesture (F1 = 0.892): two-hand gesture loses spatial context when landmarks are per-hand
   276вҶ’  - Pinch gesture (F1 = 0.919): subtle finger proximity differences
   277вҶ’- MLP handles all classes above 95% F1:
   278вҶ’  - None class (F1 = 0.953): inherently diverse (any non-gesture position)
   279вҶ’  - Frame (F1 = 0.973): same two-hand limitation but MLP compensates with richer features
   280вҶ’- MLP reduces frame confusion by 8.1 percentage points over RF
   281вҶ’
   282вҶ’**Visual:** Side-by-side confusion matrices: Left = rf_confusion_matrix.png, Right = mlp_confusion_matrix.png. Annotate the key confusion cells (frame-pinch confusion in RF, none-class errors in MLP).
   283вҶ’
   284вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Look at the frame row in the Random Forest matrix -- significant confusion with pinch and none. The MLP cleans this up dramatically. The remaining errors in the MLP are concentrated in the 'none' class, which makes sense: 'none' is a catch-all category that includes every possible non-gesture hand position."
   285вҶ’
   286вҶ’---
   287вҶ’
   288вҶ’## Slide 15: Per-Class F1 Score Comparison
   289вҶ’
   290вҶ’**Title:** Per-Class F1: MLP Dominates Every Category
   291вҶ’
   292вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   293вҶ’- MLP exceeds RF on all five gesture classes
   294вҶ’- Biggest MLP advantage: Frame (+8.1 pp) and Pinch (+7.6 pp)
   295вҶ’- Open Hand and Fist are "easy" gestures for both methods (F1 > 0.98)
   296вҶ’- The hard gestures (frame, pinch, none) are where model sophistication pays off
   297вҶ’
   298вҶ’**Breakdown:**
   299вҶ’
   300вҶ’| Class     | RF F1  | MLP F1 | Delta   |
   301вҶ’|-----------|--------|--------|---------|
   302вҶ’| open_hand | 0.985  | 0.994  | +0.009  |
   303вҶ’| fist      | 0.983  | 0.993  | +0.010  |
   304вҶ’| pinch     | 0.919  | 0.995  | +0.076  |
   305вҶ’| frame     | 0.892  | 0.973  | +0.081  |
   306вҶ’| none      | 0.919  | 0.953  | +0.034  |
   307вҶ’
   308вҶ’**Visual:** Grouped bar chart (per_class_f1_comparison.png) with RF and MLP bars side by side for each gesture class. Annotate the delta values above the bars.
   309вҶ’
   310вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "The easy gestures -- open hand and fist -- are already near-perfect for both methods. The real differentiation happens on the hard gestures. The MLP's ability to learn from the full 60-dimensional landmark vector gives it an edge on the subtle distinctions that hand-engineered distance features miss."
   311вҶ’
   312вҶ’---
   313вҶ’
   314вҶ’## Slide 16: Cross-Validation Stability
   315вҶ’
   316вҶ’**Title:** Fold Stability: MLP Generalizes Consistently
   317вҶ’
   318вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   319вҶ’- MLP: std = 0.26% across 5 folds (range: 97.89% to 98.59%)
   320вҶ’- RF: std = 1.25% across 5 folds (range: 92.09% to 95.72%)
   321вҶ’- MLP 95% CI: [98.16%, 98.64%] -- tight bounds, high confidence
   322вҶ’- RF fold 4 dip (92.09%) suggests sensitivity to specific person groups
   323вҶ’- MLP is robust to person variation -- critical for real-world deployment
   324вҶ’
   325вҶ’**Visual:** Table of fold-by-fold accuracies (from Table 6 in the report), plus a line chart showing MLP as a nearly flat line and RF as a more volatile line across 5 folds.
   326вҶ’
   327вҶ’| Fold | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP    |
   328вҶ’|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
   329вҶ’| 1    | 0.60%     | 93.45%        | 98.48% |
   330вҶ’| 2    | 0.98%     | 94.64%        | 97.89% |
   331вҶ’| 3    | 1.30%     | 94.75%        | 98.54% |
   332вҶ’| 4    | 1.25%     | 92.09%        | 98.54% |
   333вҶ’| 5    | 0.81%     | 95.72%        | 98.59% |
   334вҶ’
   335вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Accuracy on one test split can be misleading. What matters is consistency. The MLP varies by less than 0.7 percentage points across all five folds, meaning it works equally well regardless of which group of people ends up in the test set. The Random Forest dips to 92% on fold 4, suggesting some person groups have hand geometries that the 24-distance feature set does not capture well."
   336вҶ’
   337вҶ’---
   338вҶ’
   339вҶ’## Slide 17: Live Demo / Game Demo
   340вҶ’
   341вҶ’**Title:** Live Demo: Puzzle Game Controlled by Hand Gestures
   342вҶ’
   343вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   344вҶ’- Browser-based jigsaw puzzle game (HTML/CSS/JavaScript)
   345вҶ’- Real-time webcam feed processed by MediaPipe in-browser
   346вҶ’- MLP model loaded via TensorFlow.js for client-side inference
   347вҶ’- Gesture controls: Open hand (release), Fist (rotate), Pinch (grab), Frame (screenshot)
   348вҶ’- Fully client-side: no server required, works offline after loading
   349вҶ’
   350вҶ’**Demo flow:**
   351вҶ’1. Show the webcam feed with landmark overlay
   352вҶ’2. Demonstrate each gesture and its game action
   353вҶ’3. Complete a simple puzzle move (grab a piece, move it, release it)
   354вҶ’
   355вҶ’**Visual:** Screenshot of the game interface showing the puzzle on one side and the webcam feed with hand landmarks on the other. If live demo is not possible, show a screen recording or sequence of screenshots.
   356вҶ’
   357вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Let me show you the system in action. Everything runs in the browser -- MediaPipe detects my hand, extracts landmarks, the MLP classifies the gesture, and the game responds. Watch how I use pinch to grab a piece, move my hand to reposition it, then open my hand to release. The latency is imperceptible at human interaction speed."
   358вҶ’
   359вҶ’---
   360вҶ’
   361вҶ’## Slide 18: Software Engineering and Testing
   362вҶ’
   363вҶ’**Title:** Engineering Quality: 28 Tests, Modular Architecture
   364вҶ’
   365вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   366вҶ’- 28 automated tests covering preprocessing, feature extraction, all three classifiers, evaluation pipeline, and data conversion
   367вҶ’- Modular codebase: each method is a separate module with clean interfaces
   368вҶ’  - `preprocessing.py` -- shared normalization pipeline
   369вҶ’  - `features.py` -- feature extraction (angles, distances)
   370вҶ’  - `heuristic.py`, `random_forest.py`, `mlp.py` -- one file per method
   371вҶ’  - `evaluate.py` -- unified evaluation with CV, metrics, and plots
   372вҶ’  - `train.py` -- training orchestrator
   373вҶ’- Reproducible: single command runs full pipeline from data extraction to evaluation
   374вҶ’- All plots and metrics generated automatically
   375вҶ’
   376вҶ’**Visual:** Project structure tree (from Section 5.2 of report), with test coverage callout. Optionally show a terminal screenshot of `pytest` passing all 28 tests.
   377вҶ’
   378вҶ’```
   379вҶ’CVsubject/
   380вҶ’|-- ml/
   381вҶ’|   |-- preprocessing.py
   382вҶ’|   |-- features.py
   383вҶ’|   |-- heuristic.py
   384вҶ’|   |-- random_forest.py
   385вҶ’|   |-- mlp.py
   386вҶ’|   |-- evaluate.py
   387вҶ’|   |-- train.py
   388вҶ’|   |-- tests/test_core.py  (28 tests)
   389вҶ’|-- game/
   390вҶ’|   |-- index.html, game.js, gesture.js, main.js
   391вҶ’|-- models/
   392вҶ’|   |-- plots/ (6 auto-generated charts)
   393вҶ’```
   394вҶ’
   395вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "This is not just a Jupyter notebook experiment. The codebase is modular, tested, and reproducible. Every module has a single responsibility. The 28 tests ensure that preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification behave correctly even as we iterate on the models. You can reproduce every result in this presentation with one command."
   396вҶ’
   397вҶ’---
   398вҶ’
   399вҶ’## Slide 19: Limitations and Future Work
   400вҶ’
   401вҶ’**Title:** What We Would Do Next
   402вҶ’
   403вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   404вҶ’- Current Limitations:
   405вҶ’  - Heuristic needs recalibration for 2D landmarks (or re-extract with full 3D using MediaPipe Python API on raw images)
   406вҶ’  - Frame gesture is inherently limited by per-hand landmark extraction -- a two-stage frame detector analyzing spatial relationship between both hands would improve this
   407вҶ’  - MLP exceeds 60 FPS budget (43.7 ms) -- acceptable for games, not for real-time rendering pipelines
   408вҶ’- Future Work:
   409вҶ’  - Model quantization: convert MLP to TFLite or ONNX for faster browser inference via WebAssembly
   410вҶ’  - User adaptation: fine-tune on specific user's hand geometry for personalized accuracy
   411вҶ’  - Temporal modeling: LSTM or 1D-CNN over landmark frame sequences for motion-aware gesture recognition (e.g., distinguishing a wave from an open hand)
   412вҶ’  - Expanded gesture vocabulary beyond 5 classes
   413вҶ’
   414вҶ’**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left column: "Limitations" with warning icons. Right column: "Future Work" with forward-arrow icons. Each item is a short phrase, not a sentence.
   415вҶ’
   416вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "Every system has limits. Our biggest limitation is the frame gesture -- we extract landmarks per hand, so the two-hand spatial relationship is lost. The most impactful future work would be temporal modeling. Right now we classify single frames independently. A sequence model could distinguish dynamic gestures like waving from static ones like open hand, and would also smooth out frame-to-frame noise."
   417вҶ’
   418вҶ’---
   419вҶ’
   420вҶ’## Slide 20: Conclusion and Key Takeaway
   421вҶ’
   422вҶ’**Title:** One Slide Summary
   423вҶ’
   424вҶ’**Key Bullets:**
   425вҶ’- The MLP neural network is the clear winner: 98.41% accuracy, 0.26% standard deviation, all classes above 95% F1
   426вҶ’- Random Forest is the best practical tradeoff: 94.13% accuracy within the 60 FPS latency budget
   427вҶ’- The heuristic's failure (0.99%) is itself a finding: hand-crafted rules are brittle to data pipeline assumptions
   428вҶ’- Person-aware evaluation (Group K-Fold) ensures results reflect real-world generalization
   429вҶ’- The system works end-to-end: from webcam to browser-based puzzle game control
   430вҶ’
   431вҶ’**Key Takeaway (large, centered text):**
   432вҶ’> "Data-driven methods do not just outperform heuristics -- they are 100x more accurate. But the gap between classical ML and deep learning is only 4 percentage points. Choose based on your latency budget."
   433вҶ’
   434вҶ’**Visual:** Summary table (from Table 8 in report):
   435вҶ’
   436вҶ’| Criterion               | Best Method   | Score          |
   437вҶ’|--------------------------|---------------|----------------|
   438вҶ’| Overall Accuracy         | MLP           | 98.41%         |
   439вҶ’| Inference Speed          | Heuristic     | 0.1 ms         |
   440вҶ’| Accuracy/Latency Balance | Random Forest | 94.13% @ 13 ms |
   441вҶ’| Per-Class Consistency    | MLP           | All > 95% F1   |
   442вҶ’| Model Size               | Heuristic     | 170 B          |
   443вҶ’| Generalization (low std) | MLP           | +/- 0.26%      |
   444вҶ’
   445вҶ’**Speaker Notes:** "If you remember one thing from this presentation, let it be this: the jump from heuristic to data-driven is enormous -- from 1% to 94%. But the jump from classical ML to deep learning is incremental -- from 94% to 98%. That second jump costs you 3x the latency. For most real-time applications, either the Random Forest or the MLP will serve you well. The heuristic is a cautionary tale about assumptions."
   446вҶ’
   447вҶ’---
   448вҶ’
   449вҶ’## Appendix: Presentation Timing Guide
   450вҶ’
   451вҶ’| Slide(s) | Section                      | Time    | Cumulative |
   452вҶ’|-----------|------------------------------|---------|------------|
   453вҶ’| 1         | Title                        | 0:30    | 0:30       |

   466вҶ’|           | Q&A Buffer                   | 0:30    | 20:00      |
   467вҶ’
   468вҶ’---
   469вҶ’
   470вҶ’## Appendix: Visual Assets Checklist
   471вҶ’

   484вҶ’**Additional visuals to create:**

# Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
## Presentation Outline (15-20 minutes, 18 slides)

---

## Slide 1: Title Slide

**Title:** Hand Gesture Recognition for Puzzle Game Control
**Subtitle:** A Comparative Study of Heuristic, Classical ML, and Deep Learning Approaches

**Key Elements:**
- Course: Computer Vision
- Student Name / Student ID
- Date: February 2026
- University logo (if applicable)

**Visual:** Clean title layout with a composite image showing the five gesture classes (open hand, fist, pinch, frame, none) as small icons or photos across the bottom.

**Speaker Notes:** "Today I will present a hand gesture recognition system that lets you control a browser-based jigsaw puzzle using just your webcam. We compare three classification methods of increasing complexity and show why data-driven approaches dominate hand-crafted rules."

---

## Slide 2: Problem Statement and Motivation

**Title:** Why Hand Gesture Recognition?

**Key Bullets:**
- Human-computer interaction is moving beyond keyboards and mice toward natural, touchless interfaces
- Hand gestures are intuitive, require no hardware beyond a webcam, and map naturally to spatial tasks like puzzle manipulation
- Challenge: Gestures vary dramatically across individuals (hand size, skin tone, angle, lighting)
- Our goal: Build a real-time gesture classifier that generalizes to unseen users

**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left: photo of someone using a keyboard (traditional). Right: photo/illustration of someone using hand gestures to interact with a screen (natural). Arrow between them labeled "The shift."

**Speaker Notes:** "The core challenge is not just recognizing gestures from one person, but building a system that works for anyone who sits in front of the webcam. That generalization requirement drives every design decision in this project."

---

## Slide 3: Project Objectives

**Title:** Three Methods, One Question: What Works Best?

**Key Bullets:**
- Objective 1: Implement and compare three gesture classification methods of increasing complexity
  - Rule-based heuristic (no learning)
  - Classical ML: Random Forest (engineered features)
  - Deep Learning: MLP neural network (raw landmarks)
- Objective 2: Evaluate all methods using person-aware cross-validation on a large-scale public dataset
- Objective 3: Deploy the best model in a real-time browser-based puzzle game

**Visual:** Three columns showing method progression: Heuristic (hand-drawn rules icon) --> Random Forest (tree diagram icon) --> MLP (neural network icon). Arrow beneath labeled "Increasing complexity."

**Speaker Notes:** "We deliberately chose three methods at different points on the complexity spectrum. The heuristic uses zero training data. The Random Forest learns from engineered features. The MLP learns directly from raw coordinates. This lets us measure exactly what data-driven learning buys you."

---

## Slide 4: Gesture Classes and Game Mapping

**Title:** Five Gestures, Five Game Actions

**Key Bullets:**
- Open Hand (spread fingers, palm forward) --> Release puzzle piece
- Fist (closed hand) --> Rotate puzzle piece
- Pinch (thumb + index touching) --> Grab / release piece
- Frame (two hands forming rectangle) --> Take screenshot
- None (no specific gesture) --> No action

**Visual:** A 5-column strip. Each column shows: gesture photo/illustration on top, gesture name in the middle, game action icon on the bottom. Connected by arrows.

**Speaker Notes:** "These five gestures were chosen because they are visually distinct and map to natural puzzle interactions. Frame is the hardest because it requires two hands, and None is the hardest to learn because it covers everything that is not one of the other four."

---

## Slide 5: System Pipeline Overview

**Title:** End-to-End Pipeline: From Webcam to Game Control

**Key Bullets:**
- Stage 1: Video frame captured from webcam
- Stage 2: Google MediaPipe HandLandmarker extracts 21 3D landmarks per hand
- Stage 3: Preprocessing normalizes landmarks (position + scale invariant)
- Stage 4: Classifier predicts one of five gesture classes
- Stage 5: Prediction drives game action in browser

**Visual:** Reproduce the TikZ pipeline diagram from the report (Figure 5). Flow: Video Frame --> MediaPipe HandLandmarker --> 21 Landmarks (x,y,z) --> [Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP] --> Gesture Classification --> [Puzzle Game | Evaluation].

**Speaker Notes:** "The key architectural insight is that MediaPipe does the heavy lifting of hand detection and landmark extraction. Our classifiers only need to interpret 21 points in 3D space. This keeps the classification fast and lets us compare methods fairly on the same input representation."

**Equation to show:**
```
Video Frame --[MediaPipe]--> L in R^{21x3} --[Classifier]--> y in {1,...,5}
```

---

## Slide 6: Dataset -- HaGRID v2

**Title:** Training Data: HaGRID v2 Dataset

**Key Bullets:**
- HaGRID v2: 1,086,158 FullHD images, 37,583 unique subjects, 33 gesture classes
- We selected 5 classes mapped to our gestures: stop-->open_hand, fist-->fist, thumb_index-->pinch, take_picture-->frame, no_gesture-->none
- Extracted subset: 9,231 samples from 3,607 unique subjects
- Capped at 1,000 samples per gesture per split for computational efficiency
- Pre-annotated with MediaPipe landmarks (x, y only; z set to 0.0)

**Visual:** Table showing the dataset split statistics:

| Class     | Train | Val   | Total |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| open_hand | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| fist      | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| pinch     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| frame     | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| none      | 1,000 | 231   | 1,231 |
| **Total** | **5,000** | **4,231** | **9,231** |

**Speaker Notes:** "HaGRID is one of the largest public hand gesture datasets available. By using pre-annotated landmarks from the dataset authors, we ensure consistency. The slight class imbalance in 'none' reflects that fewer no-gesture samples were available in the validation split."

---

## Slide 7: Preprocessing Pipeline

**Title:** Making Landmarks Position- and Scale-Invariant

**Key Bullets:**
- Step 1: Wrist-relative translation -- subtract wrist landmark (index 0) from all 21 landmarks, centering the hand at origin
- Step 2: Scale normalization -- divide all coordinates by the Euclidean distance from wrist to middle-finger MCP (landmark 9)
- Output: 20 normalized landmarks x 3 coordinates = 60-dimensional feature vector
- This ensures the classifier sees the same representation regardless of hand position, distance from camera, or hand size

**Visual:** Diagram showing the normalization math with a hand landmark overlay:

```
l_hat_i = (l_i - l_0) / ||l_9 - l_0||_2,  for i = 1,...,20
```

Show a "before" (raw landmarks scattered on image plane) and "after" (centered at origin, unit-scaled) side by side.

**Worked Example (optional callout box):**
- Raw: l_0 = (0.45, 0.72, 0.0), l_9 = (0.42, 0.55, 0.0)
- Scale factor d = 0.1726
- All landmarks divided by d after wrist subtraction

**Speaker Notes:** "This two-step normalization is critical. Without it, the classifier would need to learn that the same gesture at different positions and scales is the same gesture. With it, we hand the classifier a canonical representation every time."

---

## Slide 8: Method 1 -- Rule-Based Heuristic

**Title:** Method 1: Hand-Crafted Rules (and Why They Failed)

**Key Bullets:**
- Approach: ~20 manually tuned thresholds on finger curl angles and key distances
- Classification logic:
  - Open Hand: all fingers extended (curl angles below threshold)
  - Fist: all fingers curled (curl angles above threshold)
  - Pinch: thumb-tip to index-tip distance below proximity threshold
  - Frame: two hands detected with specific spatial relationship
  - None: default fallback
- Result: 0.99% accuracy (worse than random chance at 20%)
- No training required, but no adaptability either

**Visual:** Flowchart of the heuristic decision tree. Annotate with a large red "FAILED" stamp or red X. Show the confusion matrix (heuristic_confusion_matrix.png) inset, demonstrating near-random predictions.

**Speaker Notes:** "The heuristic scored below random chance. This happened because the rules were designed assuming 3D landmarks, but HaGRID only provides 2D coordinates with z=0. The curl angle calculations collapse without depth information. This is the strongest argument for data-driven methods: rules that seem reasonable can fail catastrophically when assumptions about the data pipeline are wrong."

---

## Slide 9: Method 2 -- Random Forest

**Title:** Method 2: Random Forest on Engineered Distance Features

**Key Bullets:**
- 100 decision trees, max_depth=15 (scikit-learn)
- Input: 24-dimensional feature vector of pairwise Euclidean distances between key landmark pairs (fingertips, MCP joints, PIP joints)
- Semi-automatic feature engineering: distances selected to capture discriminative geometric relationships
- Model size: 8.6 MB (~100K tree nodes)
- Result: 94.13% accuracy (+/- 1.25%), 13.2 ms latency

**Visual:** Two-part visual. Left: diagram of a hand with lines drawn between the key landmark pairs used as features. Right: RF feature importance bar chart (rf_feature_importance.png) showing the top 15 most important pairwise distance features.

**Speaker Notes:** "The Random Forest jumps from 1% to 94% accuracy by learning from data instead of guessing thresholds. The 24 pairwise distance features capture the geometric relationships that matter. Notice in the feature importance plot which finger distances are most discriminative -- this gives us interpretability that the MLP lacks."

---

## Slide 10: Method 3 -- MLP Neural Network

**Title:** Method 3: MLP Neural Network on Raw Landmarks

**Key Bullets:**
- Architecture: Input(60) --> Dense(128, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(64, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(5, Softmax)
- Total parameters: 14,789 (model size: 227 KB -- 38x smaller than Random Forest)
- No manual feature engineering: learns directly from the 60-dimensional normalized landmark vector
- Training: Adam optimizer (lr=0.001), sparse categorical cross-entropy, early stopping (patience=10)
- Converged in 17/50 epochs. Train acc: 98.56%, Val acc: 97.98%
- Result: 98.41% accuracy (+/- 0.26%), 43.7 ms latency

**Visual:** Neural network architecture diagram showing the layer dimensions:

```
[60 inputs] --> [128 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [64 neurons + ReLU] --> [Dropout 0.3] --> [5 classes + Softmax]
```

Annotate with parameter counts at each layer. Optionally show MLP training curve (train/val accuracy over 17 epochs).

**Speaker Notes:** "The MLP is the simplest possible neural network -- just two hidden layers -- yet it beats the Random Forest by 4.3 percentage points. It achieves this by operating on the full 60-dimensional landmark vector rather than a hand-picked 24-dimensional distance subset. The network discovers its own features. Notice the model is only 227 KB, making it easy to deploy in a browser."

---

## Slide 11: Methods Comparison Table

**Title:** Three Methods Side-by-Side

**Key Bullets (as table):**

| Aspect              | Heuristic       | Random Forest   | MLP             |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Input Features      | Angles + dist.  | 24 pairwise dist.| 60 raw coords  |
| Feature Engineering | Manual          | Semi-automatic  | None (end-to-end)|
| Training Required   | No              | Yes (fast)      | Yes (moderate)  |
| Parameters          | ~20 thresholds  | ~100K nodes     | 14,789 weights  |
| Model Size          | 170 B           | 8.6 MB          | 227 KB          |
| Interpretability    | High            | Medium          | Low             |
| CV Accuracy         | 0.99%           | 94.13%          | 98.41%          |
| Latency             | 0.1 ms          | 13.2 ms         | 43.7 ms         |

**Visual:** Clean comparison table. Highlight the MLP row/column in green for accuracy, the Heuristic in green for speed, the RF as the middle ground. Use color coding to make trade-offs immediately visible.

**Speaker Notes:** "This table is the core deliverable of the project. Three takeaways: First, data-driven methods crush hand-crafted rules. Second, the MLP is 38x smaller than the Random Forest yet 4 points more accurate. Third, all three methods have latencies well under 1 second, but only the heuristic and RF fit within a 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms."

---

## Slide 12: Evaluation Methodology

**Title:** Person-Aware Cross-Validation: Testing on Strangers

**Key Bullets:**
- Group 5-Fold Cross-Validation using person IDs as groups (sklearn GroupKFold)
- Guarantee: No subject appears in both training and test sets within any fold
- This tests generalization to truly unseen individuals, not just unseen images of the same person
- 3,607 unique subjects ensure diverse hand shapes, skin tones, and gesture styles
- Full Leave-One-Person-Out CV was computationally impractical at this scale
- Secondary metric: stratified 80/20 split for comparison

**Visual:** Diagram showing 5 folds with person silhouette icons. Each fold clearly separates "train persons" from "test persons" with no overlap. Annotate: "Person A never appears in both train and test within the same fold."

**Speaker Notes:** "This is the most important methodological choice in the project. Standard random splitting would leak person identity into the test set -- the model could memorize a specific person's hand shape rather than learning gesture geometry. Group K-Fold eliminates that risk. Our results reflect how well the system works on someone it has never seen before."

---

## Slide 13: Results -- Accuracy vs. Latency Tradeoff

**Title:** The Accuracy-Latency Tradeoff

**Key Bullets:**
- Heuristic: 0.99% accuracy at 0.1 ms (fast but useless)
- Random Forest: 94.13% at 13.2 ms (fits within 60 FPS budget of 16.7 ms)
- MLP: 98.41% at 43.7 ms (highest accuracy, exceeds 60 FPS budget but acceptable for game control at ~23 FPS)
- For puzzle game control, 23 FPS is sufficient -- gesture changes happen at human speed (~1-2 per second)
- The 60 FPS line matters for frame-by-frame rendering, not gesture classification

**Visual:** Reproduce the scatter plot from the report (Figure 1): X-axis = Inference Latency (ms), Y-axis = CV Accuracy (%). Three labeled dots (Heuristic, RF, MLP). Dashed red vertical line at 16.7 ms labeled "60 FPS budget."

**Speaker Notes:** "This chart tells the full story. The heuristic is in the bottom-left corner -- fast but broken. The Random Forest sits just inside the 60 FPS budget. The MLP is the clear accuracy winner but crosses the 60 FPS line. For our puzzle game, this is fine. You do not need 60 gesture predictions per second. You need accurate ones."

---

## Slide 14: Confusion Matrices -- RF vs MLP

**Title:** Where Do the Models Get Confused?

**Key Bullets:**
- Random Forest struggles most with:
  - Frame gesture (F1 = 0.892): two-hand gesture loses spatial context when landmarks are per-hand
  - Pinch gesture (F1 = 0.919): subtle finger proximity differences
- MLP handles all classes above 95% F1:
  - None class (F1 = 0.953): inherently diverse (any non-gesture position)
  - Frame (F1 = 0.973): same two-hand limitation but MLP compensates with richer features
- MLP reduces frame confusion by 8.1 percentage points over RF

**Visual:** Side-by-side confusion matrices: Left = rf_confusion_matrix.png, Right = mlp_confusion_matrix.png. Annotate the key confusion cells (frame-pinch confusion in RF, none-class errors in MLP).

**Speaker Notes:** "Look at the frame row in the Random Forest matrix -- significant confusion with pinch and none. The MLP cleans this up dramatically. The remaining errors in the MLP are concentrated in the 'none' class, which makes sense: 'none' is a catch-all category that includes every possible non-gesture hand position."

---

## Slide 15: Per-Class F1 Score Comparison

**Title:** Per-Class F1: MLP Dominates Every Category

**Key Bullets:**
- MLP exceeds RF on all five gesture classes
- Biggest MLP advantage: Frame (+8.1 pp) and Pinch (+7.6 pp)
- Open Hand and Fist are "easy" gestures for both methods (F1 > 0.98)
- The hard gestures (frame, pinch, none) are where model sophistication pays off

**Breakdown:**

| Class     | RF F1  | MLP F1 | Delta   |
|-----------|--------|--------|---------|
| open_hand | 0.985  | 0.994  | +0.009  |
| fist      | 0.983  | 0.993  | +0.010  |
| pinch     | 0.919  | 0.995  | +0.076  |
| frame     | 0.892  | 0.973  | +0.081  |
| none      | 0.919  | 0.953  | +0.034  |

**Visual:** Grouped bar chart (per_class_f1_comparison.png) with RF and MLP bars side by side for each gesture class. Annotate the delta values above the bars.

**Speaker Notes:** "The easy gestures -- open hand and fist -- are already near-perfect for both methods. The real differentiation happens on the hard gestures. The MLP's ability to learn from the full 60-dimensional landmark vector gives it an edge on the subtle distinctions that hand-engineered distance features miss."

---

## Slide 16: Cross-Validation Stability

**Title:** Fold Stability: MLP Generalizes Consistently

**Key Bullets:**
- MLP: std = 0.26% across 5 folds (range: 97.89% to 98.59%)
- RF: std = 1.25% across 5 folds (range: 92.09% to 95.72%)
- MLP 95% CI: [98.16%, 98.64%] -- tight bounds, high confidence
- RF fold 4 dip (92.09%) suggests sensitivity to specific person groups
- MLP is robust to person variation -- critical for real-world deployment

**Visual:** Table of fold-by-fold accuracies (from Table 6 in the report), plus a line chart showing MLP as a nearly flat line and RF as a more volatile line across 5 folds.

| Fold | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP    |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| 1    | 0.60%     | 93.45%        | 98.48% |
| 2    | 0.98%     | 94.64%        | 97.89% |
| 3    | 1.30%     | 94.75%        | 98.54% |
| 4    | 1.25%     | 92.09%        | 98.54% |
| 5    | 0.81%     | 95.72%        | 98.59% |

**Speaker Notes:** "Accuracy on one test split can be misleading. What matters is consistency. The MLP varies by less than 0.7 percentage points across all five folds, meaning it works equally well regardless of which group of people ends up in the test set. The Random Forest dips to 92% on fold 4, suggesting some person groups have hand geometries that the 24-distance feature set does not capture well."

---

## Slide 17: Live Demo / Game Demo

**Title:** Live Demo: Puzzle Game Controlled by Hand Gestures

**Key Bullets:**
- Browser-based jigsaw puzzle game (HTML/CSS/JavaScript)
- Real-time webcam feed processed by MediaPipe in-browser
- MLP model loaded via TensorFlow.js for client-side inference
- Gesture controls: Open hand (release), Fist (rotate), Pinch (grab), Frame (screenshot)
- Fully client-side: no server required, works offline after loading

**Demo flow:**
1. Show the webcam feed with landmark overlay
2. Demonstrate each gesture and its game action
3. Complete a simple puzzle move (grab a piece, move it, release it)

**Visual:** Screenshot of the game interface showing the puzzle on one side and the webcam feed with hand landmarks on the other. If live demo is not possible, show a screen recording or sequence of screenshots.

**Speaker Notes:** "Let me show you the system in action. Everything runs in the browser -- MediaPipe detects my hand, extracts landmarks, the MLP classifies the gesture, and the game responds. Watch how I use pinch to grab a piece, move my hand to reposition it, then open my hand to release. The latency is imperceptible at human interaction speed."

---

## Slide 18: Software Engineering and Testing

**Title:** Engineering Quality: 28 Tests, Modular Architecture

**Key Bullets:**
- 28 automated tests covering preprocessing, feature extraction, all three classifiers, evaluation pipeline, and data conversion
- Modular codebase: each method is a separate module with clean interfaces
  - `preprocessing.py` -- shared normalization pipeline
  - `features.py` -- feature extraction (angles, distances)
  - `heuristic.py`, `random_forest.py`, `mlp.py` -- one file per method
  - `evaluate.py` -- unified evaluation with CV, metrics, and plots
  - `train.py` -- training orchestrator
- Reproducible: single command runs full pipeline from data extraction to evaluation
- All plots and metrics generated automatically

**Visual:** Project structure tree (from Section 5.2 of report), with test coverage callout. Optionally show a terminal screenshot of `pytest` passing all 28 tests.

```
CVsubject/
|-- ml/
|   |-- preprocessing.py
|   |-- features.py
|   |-- heuristic.py
|   |-- random_forest.py
|   |-- mlp.py
|   |-- evaluate.py
|   |-- train.py
|   |-- tests/test_core.py  (28 tests)
|-- game/
|   |-- index.html, game.js, gesture.js, main.js
|-- models/
|   |-- plots/ (6 auto-generated charts)
```

**Speaker Notes:** "This is not just a Jupyter notebook experiment. The codebase is modular, tested, and reproducible. Every module has a single responsibility. The 28 tests ensure that preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification behave correctly even as we iterate on the models. You can reproduce every result in this presentation with one command."

---

## Slide 19: Limitations and Future Work

**Title:** What We Would Do Next

**Key Bullets:**
- Current Limitations:
  - Heuristic needs recalibration for 2D landmarks (or re-extract with full 3D using MediaPipe Python API on raw images)
  - Frame gesture is inherently limited by per-hand landmark extraction -- a two-stage frame detector analyzing spatial relationship between both hands would improve this
  - MLP exceeds 60 FPS budget (43.7 ms) -- acceptable for games, not for real-time rendering pipelines
- Future Work:
  - Model quantization: convert MLP to TFLite or ONNX for faster browser inference via WebAssembly
  - User adaptation: fine-tune on specific user's hand geometry for personalized accuracy
  - Temporal modeling: LSTM or 1D-CNN over landmark frame sequences for motion-aware gesture recognition (e.g., distinguishing a wave from an open hand)
  - Expanded gesture vocabulary beyond 5 classes

**Visual:** Two-column layout. Left column: "Limitations" with warning icons. Right column: "Future Work" with forward-arrow icons. Each item is a short phrase, not a sentence.

**Speaker Notes:** "Every system has limits. Our biggest limitation is the frame gesture -- we extract landmarks per hand, so the two-hand spatial relationship is lost. The most impactful future work would be temporal modeling. Right now we classify single frames independently. A sequence model could distinguish dynamic gestures like waving from static ones like open hand, and would also smooth out frame-to-frame noise."

---

## Slide 20: Conclusion and Key Takeaway

**Title:** One Slide Summary

**Key Bullets:**
- The MLP neural network is the clear winner: 98.41% accuracy, 0.26% standard deviation, all classes above 95% F1
- Random Forest is the best practical tradeoff: 94.13% accuracy within the 60 FPS latency budget
- The heuristic's failure (0.99%) is itself a finding: hand-crafted rules are brittle to data pipeline assumptions
- Person-aware evaluation (Group K-Fold) ensures results reflect real-world generalization
- The system works end-to-end: from webcam to browser-based puzzle game control

**Key Takeaway (large, centered text):**
> "Data-driven methods do not just outperform heuristics -- they are 100x more accurate. But the gap between classical ML and deep learning is only 4 percentage points. Choose based on your latency budget."

**Visual:** Summary table (from Table 8 in report):

| Criterion               | Best Method   | Score          |
|--------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Overall Accuracy         | MLP           | 98.41%         |
| Inference Speed          | Heuristic     | 0.1 ms         |
| Accuracy/Latency Balance | Random Forest | 94.13% @ 13 ms |
| Per-Class Consistency    | MLP           | All > 95% F1   |
| Model Size               | Heuristic     | 170 B          |
| Generalization (low std) | MLP           | +/- 0.26%      |

**Speaker Notes:** "If you remember one thing from this presentation, let it be this: the jump from heuristic to data-driven is enormous -- from 1% to 94%. But the jump from classical ML to deep learning is incremental -- from 94% to 98%. That second jump costs you 3x the latency. For most real-time applications, either the Random Forest or the MLP will serve you well. The heuristic is a cautionary tale about assumptions."

---

## Appendix: Presentation Timing Guide

| Slide(s) | Section                      | Time    | Cumulative |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------|------------|
| 1         | Title                        | 0:30    | 0:30       |

|           | Q&A Buffer                   | 0:30    | 20:00      |

---

## Appendix: Visual Assets Checklist

All plots are pre-generated in `/models/plots/`:

| Slide | Visual Needed                           | Source File                                    |
|-------|-----------------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| 8     | Heuristic confusion matrix              | `models/plots/heuristic_confusion_matrix.png`  |
| 9     | RF feature importance                   | `models/plots/rf_feature_importance.png`       |

**Additional visuals to create:**
- Slide 4: Gesture class photo strip (source from HaGRID samples or take new photos)
- Slide 5: Pipeline diagram (adapt TikZ from report or recreate in slide tool)

---

## Appendix: Design Recommendations

**Color Palette:**

input.content:
# Hand Gesture Recognition for Interactive Puzzle Game Control
## Presentation Outline (20 minutes, 20 slides)

---

## Slide 1: Title

**Title:** Hand Gesture Recognition for Interactive Puzzle Game Control
**Subtitle:** A Comparative Analysis of Heuristic, Classical Machine Learning, and Neural Network Approaches

- Course: Computer Vision
- Presenter: [Student Name] -- [Student ID]
- Date: February 2026

**Visual:** Title layout with a horizontal strip of the five gesture classes (open hand, fist, pinch, frame, none) rendered as annotated sample images.

**Speaker Notes:** "This presentation introduces a hand gesture recognition system that enables webcam-based control of a browser jigsaw puzzle. We evaluate three classification approaches of increasing complexity and demonstrate why data-driven methods substantially outperform hand-crafted heuristics."

---

## Slide 2: Problem Statement

**Title:** Motivation: Toward Natural Human-Computer Interaction

- Traditional input devices (keyboard, mouse) impose a barrier between user intent and system response
- Hand gestures offer an intuitive, hardware-free alternative -- requiring only a standard webcam
- Core challenge: gesture appearance varies significantly across individuals due to hand morphology, skin tone, viewing angle, and illumination
- Research question: Can we build a real-time gesture classifier that generalizes reliably to previously unseen users?

**Visual:** Side-by-side comparison -- traditional input (keyboard/mouse) vs. natural gesture interaction. Connecting arrow labeled "Toward touchless interfaces."

**Speaker Notes:** "The fundamental challenge is not merely recognizing gestures from a single user, but constructing a system that performs consistently for any user. This generalization requirement informs every architectural and methodological decision throughout the project."

---

## Slide 3: Research Objectives

**Title:** Objectives: Three Methods, One Benchmark

- **Objective 1:** Implement and compare three gesture classification methods spanning the complexity spectrum
  - Rule-based heuristic (no training data)
  - Random Forest (engineered features)
  - Multi-Layer Perceptron (raw landmark coordinates)
- **Objective 2:** Evaluate all methods using person-aware cross-validation on a large-scale public dataset
- **Objective 3:** Deploy the highest-performing model in a real-time browser-based application

**Visual:** Three-column progression diagram: Heuristic (rules icon) --> Random Forest (ensemble tree icon) --> MLP (network icon). Axis label: "Increasing model complexity."

**Speaker Notes:** "We deliberately selected three methods at distinct points on the complexity spectrum. The heuristic requires zero training data, the Random Forest learns from engineered features, and the MLP operates on raw coordinates. This design enables precise measurement of the value that data-driven learning provides."

---

## Slide 4: Gesture Vocabulary and Game Mapping

**Title:** Five Gesture Classes, Five Game Actions

| Gesture | Description | Game Action |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| Open Hand | Spread fingers, palm forward | Release puzzle piece |
| Fist | Closed hand | Rotate puzzle piece |
| Pinch | Thumb and index finger contact | Grab / release piece |
| Frame | Two hands forming a rectangle | Capture puzzle image |
| None | No defined gesture | No action |

**Visual:** Five-column strip: gesture illustration (top), class label (center), corresponding game action icon (bottom), connected by directional arrows.

**Speaker Notes:** "These five gestures were selected for their visual distinctiveness and natural mapping to puzzle manipulation tasks. The 'frame' gesture presents the greatest recognition challenge as it requires two-hand coordination, while 'none' is inherently difficult due to its role as a catch-all negative class."

---

## Slide 5: System Architecture

**Title:** End-to-End Pipeline: Webcam to Game Control

1. **Input Acquisition:** Video frame captured from webcam
2. **Landmark Extraction:** Google MediaPipe HandLandmarker produces 21 3D landmarks per detected hand
3. **Normalization:** Position- and scale-invariant preprocessing
4. **Classification:** Gesture prediction via one of three evaluated methods
5. **Application:** Predicted gesture triggers corresponding game action

**Formal notation:**

```
Video Frame --[MediaPipe]--> L вҲҲ R^{21Г—3} --[Classifier]--> y вҲҲ {1, ..., 5}
```

**Visual:** Reproduce the pipeline diagram from the report (Figure 5): Video Frame --> MediaPipe --> 21 Landmarks --> [Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP] --> Gesture Class --> [Puzzle Game | Evaluation].

**Speaker Notes:** "MediaPipe performs the computationally intensive work of hand detection and landmark extraction. Our classifiers operate solely on 21 spatial points, which keeps inference fast and enables fair comparison across methods using identical input representations."

---

## Slide 6: Dataset -- HaGRID v2

**Title:** Training Data: The HaGRID v2 Dataset

- **Source:** HaGRID v2 -- 1,086,158 FullHD images, 37,583 subjects, 33 gesture classes
- **Class mapping:** stop --> open_hand, fist --> fist, thumb_index --> pinch, take_picture --> frame, no_gesture --> none
- **Extracted subset:** 9,231 samples from 3,607 unique subjects
- **Sampling:** Capped at 1,000 samples per gesture per split
- **Landmark format:** Pre-annotated 2D coordinates (x, y); depth coordinate z = 0.0

| Class | Train | Validation | Total |
|-----------|-------|------------|-------|
| open_hand | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| fist | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| pinch | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| frame | 1,000 | 1,000 | 2,000 |
| none | 1,000 | 231 | 1,231 |
| **Total** | **5,000** | **4,231** | **9,231** |

**Speaker Notes:** "HaGRID is among the largest publicly available hand gesture datasets. By utilizing pre-annotated landmarks provided by the dataset authors, we ensure annotation consistency. The minor class imbalance in 'none' reflects limited availability of no-gesture samples in the validation partition."

---

## Slide 7: Preprocessing

**Title:** Landmark Normalization: Achieving Position and Scale Invariance

- **Step 1 -- Translation:** Subtract wrist landmark (index 0) from all 21 landmarks, centering the hand at the origin
- **Step 2 -- Scaling:** Divide all coordinates by the Euclidean distance from wrist to middle-finger MCP (landmark 9)
- **Output:** 20 normalized landmarks x 3 coordinates = **60-dimensional feature vector**

**Normalization formula:**

```
lМӮ_i = (l_i - l_0) / ||l_9 - l_0||вӮӮ,  for i = 1, ..., 20
```

**Worked example:**
- Raw: lвӮҖ = (0.45, 0.72, 0.0), lвӮү = (0.42, 0.55, 0.0)
- Scale factor d = 0.1726
- All landmarks divided by d after wrist subtraction

**Visual:** Side-by-side "before" (raw landmarks on image plane) and "after" (centered, unit-scaled) diagram.

**Speaker Notes:** "This two-step normalization is essential. Without it, the classifier must learn that identical gestures at different spatial positions and scales represent the same class. With normalization, we present a canonical representation every time, significantly reducing the learning burden."

---

## Slide 8: Method 1 -- Rule-Based Heuristic

**Title:** Method 1: Rule-Based Heuristic Classification

- **Approach:** Approximately 20 manually calibrated thresholds on finger curl angles and inter-landmark distances
- **Decision logic:**
  - Open Hand: all fingers extended (curl angles below threshold)
  - Fist: all fingers curled (curl angles above threshold)
  - Pinch: thumb-tip to index-tip distance below proximity threshold
  - Frame: two hands detected with specific spatial configuration
  - None: default classification
- **Result:** **0.99% accuracy** -- below random baseline of 20%
- **Root cause:** Rules assumed 3D landmarks; HaGRID provides only 2D (z = 0), causing angular computations to collapse

**Visual:** Heuristic decision flowchart annotated with failure indicator. Inset: heuristic confusion matrix showing near-random predictions.

**Speaker Notes:** "The heuristic scored below random chance. The rules were designed for 3D landmarks, but the dataset provides only 2D coordinates with z = 0. Curl angle calculations degenerate without depth information. This result powerfully illustrates the fragility of hand-crafted rules when underlying data assumptions are violated."

---

## Slide 9: Method 2 -- Random Forest

**Title:** Method 2: Random Forest with Engineered Distance Features

- **Configuration:** 100 decision trees, max_depth = 15 (scikit-learn)
- **Input:** 24-dimensional feature vector of pairwise Euclidean distances between discriminative landmark pairs (fingertips, MCP joints, PIP joints)
- **Feature engineering:** Semi-automatic selection of distances capturing key geometric relationships
- **Model size:** 8.6 MB (~100K tree nodes)
- **Result:** **94.13% accuracy** (+/- 1.25%), **13.2 ms** inference latency

**Visual:** Left: hand diagram with feature distance lines. Right: feature importance bar chart (top 15 pairwise distances).

**Speaker Notes:** "The Random Forest achieves a 93-point accuracy improvement over the heuristic by learning from data rather than relying on manually specified thresholds. The 24 pairwise distance features capture the geometric relationships that distinguish gestures. The feature importance visualization provides interpretability that neural networks lack."

---

## Slide 10: Method 3 -- MLP Neural Network

**Title:** Method 3: Multi-Layer Perceptron on Raw Landmarks

- **Architecture:**
  - Input(60) --> Dense(128, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(64, ReLU) --> Dropout(0.3) --> Dense(5, Softmax)
- **Parameters:** 14,789 total (model size: 227 KB -- 38x smaller than Random Forest)
- **Training:** Adam optimizer (lr = 0.001), sparse categorical cross-entropy, early stopping (patience = 10)
- **Convergence:** 17 of 50 epochs. Training accuracy: 98.56%, Validation accuracy: 97.98%
- **Result:** **98.41% accuracy** (+/- 0.26%), **43.7 ms** inference latency

**Visual:** Layer-by-layer architecture diagram with parameter counts annotated at each stage.

**Speaker Notes:** "The MLP -- the simplest possible neural network architecture with just two hidden layers -- surpasses the Random Forest by 4.3 percentage points. It achieves this by operating on the complete 60-dimensional landmark vector rather than a manually selected 24-dimensional distance subset. The network discovers its own discriminative features. At only 227 KB, the model is readily deployable in a browser environment."

---

## Slide 11: Comparative Summary

**Title:** Three Methods: Head-to-Head Comparison

| Criterion | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP |
|---------------------|-----------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Input Representation| Angles + distances | 24 pairwise distances | 60 raw coordinates |
| Feature Engineering | Manual | Semi-automatic | None (end-to-end) |
| Training Required | No | Yes (fast) | Yes (moderate) |
| Parameters | ~20 thresholds | ~100K nodes | 14,789 weights |
| Model Size | 170 B | 8.6 MB | 227 KB |
| Interpretability | High | Medium | Low |
| CV Accuracy | 0.99% | 94.13% | **98.41%** |
| Inference Latency | 0.1 ms | 13.2 ms | 43.7 ms |

**Speaker Notes:** "This comparison table encapsulates the project's central findings. Three key observations: First, data-driven methods dramatically outperform hand-crafted rules. Second, the MLP is 38x smaller than the Random Forest yet 4 points more accurate. Third, all methods operate well under one second, but only the heuristic and Random Forest meet a 60 FPS latency budget of 16.7 ms."

---

## Slide 12: Evaluation Methodology

**Title:** Person-Aware Cross-Validation: Ensuring Generalization

- **Method:** Group 5-Fold Cross-Validation with person IDs as group identifiers (sklearn GroupKFold)
- **Guarantee:** No individual appears in both training and test partitions within any fold
- **Rationale:** Standard random splits risk identity leakage -- the model may memorize individual hand morphology rather than learning gesture geometry
- **Scale:** 3,607 unique subjects ensure diversity in hand shape, skin tone, and gesture style
- **Alternative considered:** Leave-One-Person-Out CV (computationally prohibitive at this scale)

**Visual:** Diagram of 5 folds with person silhouettes, clearly separating train and test populations with no overlap. Annotation: "Person A never appears in both train and test within the same fold."

**Speaker Notes:** "This is the most consequential methodological decision in the project. Standard random splitting would allow person identity to leak into the test set. Group K-Fold eliminates that risk entirely. Our reported accuracies reflect how well the system performs on individuals it has never encountered during training."

---

## Slide 13: Results -- Accuracy vs. Latency

**Title:** The Accuracy-Latency Tradeoff

- Heuristic: 0.99% accuracy at 0.1 ms -- fast but non-functional
- Random Forest: 94.13% at 13.2 ms -- within 60 FPS budget (16.7 ms threshold)
- MLP: 98.41% at 43.7 ms -- highest accuracy, effective rate ~23 FPS
- For interactive game control, 23 FPS is sufficient: gesture transitions occur at human speed (~1--2 per second)

**Visual:** Scatter plot (accuracy vs. latency): three labeled data points with a dashed vertical line at 16.7 ms marking the 60 FPS threshold.

**Speaker Notes:** "This visualization captures the full story. The heuristic occupies the bottom-left quadrant -- fast but non-functional. The Random Forest sits at the 60 FPS boundary. The MLP achieves the highest accuracy at a modest latency cost that remains imperceptible for interactive game control. For our application, classification accuracy matters far more than raw frame rate."

---

## Slide 14: Error Analysis -- Confusion Matrices

**Title:** Error Distribution: Where Do Models Fail?

- **Random Forest weaknesses:**
  - Frame gesture (F1 = 0.892): two-hand spatial context lost in per-hand landmark extraction
  - Pinch gesture (F1 = 0.919): subtle fingertip proximity differences
- **MLP performance:**
  - All classes exceed 95% F1
  - None class (F1 = 0.953): inherently diverse negative category
  - Frame (F1 = 0.973): 8.1 percentage point improvement over Random Forest
- MLP reduces frame-class confusion by **8.1 pp** compared to Random Forest

**Visual:** Side-by-side confusion matrices (RF left, MLP right) with key confusion cells annotated.

**Speaker Notes:** "Examining the frame row in the Random Forest confusion matrix reveals significant misclassification as pinch and none. The MLP substantially reduces these errors. Residual MLP errors concentrate in the 'none' class, which is expected given its nature as a heterogeneous catch-all category encompassing every non-gesture hand configuration."

---

## Slide 15: Per-Class Performance

**Title:** Per-Class F1 Scores: MLP Advantage Across All Categories

| Class | RF F1 | MLP F1 | Improvement |
|-----------|-------|--------|-------------|
| open_hand | 0.985 | 0.994 | +0.009 |
| fist | 0.983 | 0.993 | +0.010 |
| pinch | 0.919 | 0.995 | **+0.076** |
| frame | 0.892 | 0.973 | **+0.081** |
| none | 0.919 | 0.953 | +0.034 |

- Open hand and fist are well-separated classes for both methods (F1 > 0.98)
- The largest MLP gains occur on the most challenging classes: frame (+8.1 pp), pinch (+7.6 pp)

**Visual:** Grouped bar chart with RF and MLP bars per class, delta values annotated.

**Speaker Notes:** "The straightforward gestures -- open hand and fist -- are near-perfect for both methods. The critical differentiation occurs on the challenging classes. The MLP's access to the full 60-dimensional landmark vector provides an advantage for the subtle geometric distinctions that the 24-dimensional engineered feature set does not fully capture."

---

## Slide 16: Cross-Validation Stability

**Title:** Generalization Consistency Across Folds

| Fold | Heuristic | Random Forest | MLP |
|------|-----------|---------------|--------|
| 1 | 0.60% | 93.45% | 98.48% |
| 2 | 0.98% | 94.64% | 97.89% |
| 3 | 1.30% | 94.75% | 98.54% |
| 4 | 1.25% | 92.09% | 98.54% |
| 5 | 0.81% | 95.72% | 98.59% |

- **MLP:** std = 0.26%, range = [97.89%, 98.59%], 95% CI = [98.16%, 98.64%]
- **RF:** std = 1.25%, range = [92.09%, 95.72%] -- fold 4 dip suggests sensitivity to specific subject groups
- MLP demonstrates robust generalization regardless of test population composition

**Visual:** Line chart showing MLP as a near-flat trajectory and RF as a more variable line across 5 folds.

**Speaker Notes:** "Single-split accuracy can be misleading. What matters is consistency across different test populations. The MLP varies by less than 0.7 percentage points across all five folds, confirming that it generalizes equally well regardless of which individuals constitute the test set. The Random Forest dips to 92% on fold 4, indicating that certain hand geometries fall outside what the 24-distance feature set adequately represents."

---

## Slide 17: Live Demonstration

**Title:** System Demonstration: Real-Time Gesture-Controlled Puzzle

- **Platform:** Browser-based application (HTML5 Canvas, JavaScript ES6)
- **Inference stack:** MediaPipe WASM (landmark detection) + ONNX Runtime Web (MLP classification)
- **Architecture:** Fully client-side -- no backend server, functions offline after initial load
- **Temporal smoothing:** Debounce buffer prevents gesture jitter

**Demonstration sequence:**
1. Webcam feed with real-time landmark overlay
2. Each gesture class demonstrated with corresponding game response
3. Complete puzzle interaction: grab piece (pinch) --> reposition (hand movement) --> release (open hand)

**Visual:** Game interface screenshot showing puzzle canvas alongside webcam feed with landmark skeleton overlay.

**Speaker Notes:** "The entire system runs in the browser. MediaPipe detects the hand and extracts landmarks, the ONNX-exported MLP classifies the gesture, and the game responds accordingly. The latency is imperceptible at human interaction speed. Let me demonstrate each gesture and its corresponding game action."

---

## Slide 18: Software Engineering

**Title:** Implementation Quality: Modular Architecture and Automated Testing

- **Test coverage:** 28 automated tests spanning preprocessing, feature extraction, all three classifiers, evaluation pipeline, and data conversion
- **Modular design:** Single-responsibility modules with clean interfaces
- **Reproducibility:** Single command executes the full pipeline from data extraction through evaluation
- **Automated outputs:** All plots and metrics generated programmatically

```
CVsubject/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ ml/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ preprocessing.py      # Shared normalization
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ features.py           # Feature extraction
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ heuristic.py          # Method 1
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ random_forest.py      # Method 2
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ mlp.py                # Method 3
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ evaluate.py           # Unified evaluation
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ train.py              # Training orchestrator
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ tests/test_core.py    # 28 automated tests
в”ңв”Җв”Җ game/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ index.html, game.js, gesture.js, main.js
в””в”Җв”Җ models/
    в””в”Җв”Җ plots/                # 6 auto-generated charts
```

**Speaker Notes:** "This is a production-quality codebase, not a notebook experiment. Each module has a single responsibility with well-defined interfaces. The 28 automated tests ensure correctness of preprocessing, feature extraction, and classification as the codebase evolves. Every result in this presentation is reproducible with a single command."

---

## Slide 19: Limitations and Future Directions

**Title:** Current Limitations and Proposed Extensions

**Limitations:**
- Heuristic requires recalibration for 2D-only landmark inputs (or re-extraction with full 3D via MediaPipe Python API on raw images)
- Frame gesture accuracy is constrained by per-hand landmark extraction -- spatial relationship between hands is lost
- MLP inference (43.7 ms) exceeds the 60 FPS budget -- acceptable for game interaction, insufficient for high-frequency rendering pipelines

**Future Directions:**
- **Model optimization:** Quantization via TFLite or ONNX for accelerated browser inference
- **User adaptation:** Fine-tuning on individual hand geometry for personalized accuracy
- **Temporal modeling:** LSTM or 1D-CNN over landmark frame sequences to enable motion-aware classification (e.g., distinguishing a wave from a static open hand)
- **Vocabulary expansion:** Extension beyond 5 gesture classes for richer interaction

**Visual:** Two-column layout: "Current Limitations" (left, with constraint indicators) and "Future Directions" (right, with forward-arrow indicators).

**Speaker Notes:** "The most significant limitation is the frame gesture: per-hand landmark extraction discards the spatial relationship between hands. The highest-impact future work would be temporal modeling -- classifying landmark sequences rather than individual frames. This would enable dynamic gesture recognition and inherently smooth frame-to-frame noise."

---

## Slide 20: Conclusion

**Title:** Summary and Key Findings

| Criterion | Best Method | Result |
|--------------------------|---------------|----------------|
| Overall Accuracy | MLP | 98.41% |
| Inference Speed | Heuristic | 0.1 ms |
| Accuracy/Latency Balance | Random Forest | 94.13% @ 13 ms |
| Per-Class Consistency | MLP | All > 95% F1 |
| Model Size | Heuristic | 170 B |
| Generalization Stability | MLP | +/- 0.26% std |

**Key findings:**
- The MLP achieves **98.41% accuracy** with consistent generalization (std = 0.26%) and all classes above 95% F1
- The Random Forest offers the optimal accuracy-latency tradeoff at **94.13% within the 60 FPS budget**
- The heuristic's failure (0.99%) demonstrates the brittleness of hand-crafted rules under violated data assumptions
- Person-aware evaluation ensures reported results reflect real-world generalization capability
- The system operates end-to-end: from webcam input to browser-based puzzle game control

**Central takeaway:**

> "Data-driven methods outperform heuristics by two orders of magnitude. However, the gap between classical ML and deep learning is only 4 percentage points. The choice between them should be governed by latency constraints."

**Speaker Notes:** "If there is one conclusion to retain from this presentation, it is this: the transition from heuristic to data-driven methods yields an enormous accuracy gain -- from 1% to 94%. The subsequent gain from classical ML to deep learning is incremental -- from 94% to 98% -- at the cost of 3x latency. For most real-time applications, either the Random Forest or the MLP will perform effectively. The heuristic serves as a cautionary example about the consequences of violated assumptions."

---

## Appendix A: Presentation Timing Guide

| Slide(s) | Section | Duration | Cumulative |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------|------------|
| 1 | Title | 0:30 | 0:30 |

| 19 | Limitations + Future Work | 1:00 | 18:30 |
| 20 | Conclusion | 1:00 | 19:30 |
| -- | Q&A Buffer | 0:30 | 20:00 |

---

## Appendix B: Visual Assets

## Appendix C: Design Guidelines
